proposals.title: Clinical and Microbiological Predictors of Failure for Meropenem-Vaborbactam Monotherapy in Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Infections: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a first-line agent for serious carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections, but emerging pre-2026 reports suggest treatment failures, particularly with specific resistance mechanisms (e.g., metallo-β-lactamases) or high-inoculum sites. Defining the scenarios where MEV monotherapy is insufficient is critical to guide initial combination therapy, improve outcomes, and preserve this novel antibiotic's utility in an era of escalating resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MEV monotherapy failure is associated with specific infection sources (e.g., pneumonia, intra-abdominal), high baseline severity of illness, and the presence of non-KPC carbapenemase genes (e.g., OXA-48-like, MBLs) in the infecting CRE isolate.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of hospitalized adults with CRE infections treated with MEV for ≥72 hours. The primary outcome is clinical failure (composite of 30-day mortality, microbiologic persistence, or recurrence). We will collect detailed patient, treatment, and microbiologic data, including whole-genome sequencing of isolates to identify resistance mechanisms. Multivariable logistic regression will identify independent predictors of failure. A secondary analysis will compare outcomes of patients who received initial combination therapy (MEV + another active agent) versus monotherapy in high-risk subgroups identified.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence-based criteria to identify patients with CRE infections at high risk for MEV monotherapy failure, enabling more personalized, effective initial regimens. It will also characterize the molecular epidemiology of MEV-resistant CRE in clinical practice, informing antimicrobial stewardship and surveillance priorities.
proposals.novelty: While MEV's efficacy is established, systematic analysis of its failure predictors in real-world, diverse CRE infections is lacking. This study integrates detailed clinical data with advanced microbiologic characterization to move beyond the binary 'MEV works' paradigm and define precise scenarios requiring combination therapy.

proposals.title: Beyond 30-Day Mortality: A Prospective Cohort Study of 90-Day Mortality and Functional Outcomes in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections are a critical public health threat with high short-term mortality. However, clinical trials and observational studies often use 30-day all-cause mortality as the primary endpoint, which may fail to capture the full burden of these infections. In the pre-2026 context of limited effective antibiotics, understanding longer-term survival and functional recovery is essential to evaluate the true impact of therapies and guide patient-centered care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that 90-day mortality is significantly higher than 30-day mortality in patients with CRE bloodstream infections, and that a substantial proportion of survivors experience significant, persistent functional decline as measured by the modified Rankin Scale (mRS).
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study at a large safety-net hospital. Adult patients with a monomicrobial CRE bloodstream infection will be enrolled. The primary outcomes are 90-day all-cause mortality and functional status (mRS) at discharge and 90 days. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to identify predictors of 90-day mortality, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities (Charlson Comorbidity Index), source of bacteremia, and initial appropriate antibiotic therapy. Logistic regression will model predictors of poor functional outcome (mRS 3-6).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, patient-centered data on the long-term consequences of CRE bacteremia, moving beyond traditional short-term endpoints. It will inform clinicians and patients about realistic prognoses and highlight the need for rehabilitation and follow-up care. The findings will also advocate for the inclusion of longer-term and functional outcomes in future trials of antimicrobial agents for resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Ortwine and others has focused on 30-day outcomes and resistance patterns, this study systematically evaluates the understudied 90-day horizon and incorporates a standardized functional assessment, offering a more comprehensive view of patient recovery after a severe resistant infection.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study Comparing Clinical Outcomes of Meropenem-Vaborbactam (MEV) versus Ceftazidime-Avibactam and Imipenem-Cilastatin-Relebactam for the Treatment of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections are a critical public health threat with high mortality and limited treatment options. The introduction of novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor (BL/BLI) combinations like meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV), ceftazidime-avibactam (CZA), and imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam (IMI-REL) has been a major advance, but direct comparative effectiveness data in real-world clinical settings are scarce. Determining if meaningful differences in efficacy or safety exist between these newer agents is essential to guide optimal, patient-centered therapy and antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there will be no significant difference in the primary composite outcome of 30-day all-cause mortality or clinical failure between patients treated with MEV and those treated with CZA or IMI-REL for serious CRE infections. We further hypothesize that the safety profiles, particularly regarding nephrotoxicity, will differ among the regimens.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study across three large academic safety-net hospitals. Adult patients (≥18 years) with a culture-confirmed CRE infection (e.g., bacteremia, pneumonia, UTI) treated with MEV, CZA, or IMI-REL for ≥72 hours between January 2020 and December 2025 will be included. The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day all-cause mortality or clinical failure (persistent signs/symptoms, microbiologic failure, or change in antibiotic). Secondary outcomes include individual components of the composite, nephrotoxicity, and hospital length of stay. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders such as source of infection, severity of illness (APACHE-II), renal function, and concomitant antibiotics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest direct comparative effectiveness analysis of the three leading novel BL/BLI agents for CRE to date. It will generate crucial real-world evidence to inform institutional treatment guidelines, antimicrobial stewardship protocols, and future randomized trial design. The findings will help clinicians tailor therapy based on efficacy, safety, and local resistance patterns.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have described outcomes for individual agents, this work directly compares all three next-generation BL/BLI combinations head-to-head in a heterogeneous, real-world patient population, including those with complex comorbidities typical of safety-net hospitals. The analysis will also evaluate differential safety signals, an area with limited comparative data.

proposals.title: Long-term Safety and Microbiologic Outcomes of Oral MEV Therapy for Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The novel tetracycline antibiotic MEV (mevacycline) shows promise against multidrug-resistant pathogens, but its long-term safety profile and potential to select for resistance in real-world settings are poorly characterized. This is critical as MEV may be used for extended oral courses following IV therapy, a strategy increasingly employed to facilitate hospital discharge and manage the growing burden of resistant infections in the community.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extended oral MEV therapy for resistant Gram-negative infections will be well-tolerated with a low incidence of serious adverse events, but will be associated with a measurable, quantifiable risk of developing microbiologic resistance in colonizing flora during and up to 6 months post-therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, single-center cohort study enrolling patients prescribed ≥7 days of oral MEV for infections caused by carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales or Acinetobacter baumannii. Primary outcomes are (1) incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (graded by CTCAE v5.0) and (2) emergence of MEV resistance (MIC >4 µg/mL) in baseline pathogens or colonizing gut/skin flora. Serial rectal and skin swabs will be collected at baseline, end-of-therapy, and 1, 3, and 6 months post-therapy. Isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing to identify resistance mechanisms and assess clonality. Statistical analysis will use Kaplan-Meier estimates for safety outcomes and logistic regression to identify risk factors for resistance emergence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, prospective data on the safety and resistance selection pressure of oral MEV, directly informing outpatient antibiotic stewardship. It will establish a benchmark for the risk of resistance development, crucial for guiding the placement of MEV in treatment pathways for multidrug-resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies focus on short-term efficacy, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed safety monitoring with high-resolution genomic surveillance of both pathogenic and commensal microbiota over an extended follow-up period to holistically assess the long-term ecologic impact of a new antibiotic.

proposals.title: In Vitro Synergy Testing of Meropenem-Vaborbactam (MEV) in Combination with Novel and Legacy Agents Against Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical threat to global health, with limited and often suboptimal treatment options. Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a key newer agent, but resistance and treatment failures are emerging, necessitating exploration of rational combination therapies to enhance efficacy and prevent resistance. Systematic in vitro synergy data is a crucial first step to inform future clinical studies and salvage regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: MEV will demonstrate in vitro synergistic activity when combined with specific non-beta-lactam antibiotics (e.g., polymyxins, tigecycline, aminoglycosides, or novel agents like eravacycline) against a collection of CRE isolates, including those with reduced susceptibility to MEV.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic in vitro synergy screen using a checkerboard broth microdilution method against a diverse panel of 50 clinically derived CRE isolates (including KPC, OXA-48-like, and NDM producers). MEV will be tested in combination with 6-8 partner antibiotics selected based on mechanism and clinical relevance. Synergy will be quantitatively assessed using the Fractional Inhibitory Concentration Index (FICI), with FICI ≤0.5 defining synergy. Time-kill assays will be performed on a subset of promising combinations to confirm and characterize bactericidal activity.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate a foundational dataset mapping the synergistic potential of MEV-based combinations against CRE, identifying the most promising partner drugs. It will provide a mechanistic rationale for specific salvage regimens and guide the design of future clinical studies, particularly for complex infections like hospital-acquired pneumonia or bacteremia where monotherapy may fail.
proposals.novelty: While MEV's clinical efficacy is established, systematic in vitro synergy testing against a diverse CRE panel, especially including isolates with elevated MEV MICs, is not comprehensively reported. This work directly bridges the gap between clinical observation of resistance (as seen in Dr. Ortwine's prior work) and preclinical testing of combination strategies.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Non-Carbapenem Regimens versus Carbapenems for Complicated Intra-Abdominal Infections: A Pragmatic, Propensity-Score Matched Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenems are ubiquitously used as the superior comparator in clinical trials for complicated intra-abdominal infections (cIAI), reinforcing their status as the 'gold standard' and potentially stifling the perceived value of newer, narrower-spectrum agents. This paradigm persists despite pre-2026 evidence suggesting efficacy of alternative beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations and concerns over carbapenem overuse driving resistance. A direct, real-world comparison is needed to challenge this assumption and inform more nuanced treatment guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with cIAI, initial treatment with guideline-recommended, non-carbapenem beta-lactam-based regimens (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam) is non-inferior to carbapenems in terms of clinical cure and 30-day readmission, after adjusting for severity and resistance risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity-score matched cohort study at a large safety-net hospital system. Adult patients hospitalized with cIAI (2019-2025) will be identified via ICD-10 codes and chart review. The primary exposure is initial empiric antibiotic regimen (carbapenem vs. non-carbapenem). The primary outcome is composite clinical failure (death, reoperation, or antibiotic change due to lack of efficacy). Using logistic regression, we will generate propensity scores based on demographics, comorbidities, severity markers (e.g., sepsis), and prior culture/hospitalization data, then perform 1:1 matching. Multivariable conditional logistic regression will assess the association between regimen and outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the comparative effectiveness of carbapenem-sparing regimens for cIAI, directly informing empiric therapy choices. It will challenge the default use of carbapenems as the sole benchmark in trial design and support antimicrobial stewardship efforts by validating effective alternatives.
proposals.novelty: This work directly tests the foundational assumption of carbapenem superiority in cIAI using a pragmatic, real-world design focused on patient-centered outcomes, moving beyond regulatory trial constructs. It leverages Dr. Ortwine's expertise in hospital-based studies of resistant pathogens and oral stepdown to address a core clinical dogma.

proposals.title: Comparative Impact of Microbiological Eradication versus Clinical Cure on Long-Term Outcomes in Gram-Negative Bacteremia: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 era of rising antimicrobial resistance, antibiotic trials often use microbiological endpoints like eradication as surrogates for clinical success. However, it is unclear whether achieving microbiological eradication, especially in the context of potential resistance suppression, translates to better long-term patient outcomes compared to clinical cure alone. This knowledge gap is critical for designing trials and stewardship programs that prioritize patient-centered results.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with Gram-negative bacteremia achieving clinical cure, the absence of microbiological eradication (or the emergence of resistance during therapy) is not associated with an increased risk of bacteremia recurrence or all-cause mortality within 90 days compared to those with confirmed eradication.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study at a large safety-net hospital, enrolling adults with monomicrobial Gram-negative bacteremia (2018-2024) who received definitive antibiotic therapy. The primary exposure will be microbiological outcome (eradication vs. persistence/non-documented cure) and resistance emergence on therapy, determined via structured data extraction from the electronic health record and microbiology database. The primary composite endpoint is 90-day bacteremia recurrence or all-cause mortality. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for key confounders including source control, Pitt bacteremia score, immunocompromised status, and definitive antibiotic regimen, to assess the independent association of microbiological outcomes with the long-term endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, real-world evidence on the relative importance of microbiological versus clinical endpoints, directly informing the design of future antibiotic clinical trials and stewardship guidelines. It will challenge or validate the use of eradication as a critical trial endpoint and offer a patient-centered framework for evaluating antibiotic therapy success in the resistance era.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on short-term clinical failure, this study uniquely isolates and compares the long-term prognostic value of microbiological outcomes specifically among patients who have already achieved clinical cure, a clinically relevant but understudied population.

proposals.title: Comparative Impact of Nausea Versus Other Common Adverse Events on Antibiotic Treatment Adherence and Quality of Life: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: In antibiotic therapy, adverse events (AEs) like nephrotoxicity or Clostridioides difficile infection are prioritized in clinical decision-making, while nausea is often considered minor. However, patient-reported outcomes and adherence are critical for treatment success, especially in oral step-down or outpatient therapy for conditions like bloodstream infections or community-acquired pneumonia. The relative impact of nausea on these outcomes compared to other AEs is poorly quantified, potentially leading to suboptimal patient management.
proposals.hypothesis: Nausea, frequently dismissed as a low-grade AE, has a disproportionately negative impact on patient-reported quality of life and self-reported antibiotic adherence compared to other common, non-life-threatening adverse events.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of hospitalized patients transitioning to oral antibiotic therapy for bacterial infections. Using validated instruments (e.g., the EQ-5D-5L for quality of life and the Medication Adherence Report Scale), we will collect patient-reported data at baseline, during therapy, and post-treatment. We will employ multivariable logistic and linear regression models, adjusting for infection type, pathogen resistance, and patient demographics, to compare the effects of nausea versus other AEs (e.g., diarrhea, rash, taste disturbance) on adherence and quality-of-life scores. A sensitivity analysis will use propensity score matching to control for confounding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence to refine antibiotic stewardship by quantifying the patient-centered burden of nausea. It will challenge the hierarchical classification of AEs in treatment guidelines and inform the design of supportive care protocols and patient counseling strategies to improve real-world adherence, particularly in oral regimens for resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior research catalogues AE frequency, this study directly compares the patient-centric impact of nausea against other AEs using prospective, longitudinal quality-of-life and adherence data, moving beyond clinician-centric severity grading.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Outcomes and Resistance Emergence in Patients Transitioned from Intravenous to Oral Beta-Lactam Antibiotics for Gram-Negative Bacteremia: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The shift to oral stepdown therapy, particularly for severe Gram-negative infections, is a key strategy for antimicrobial stewardship and early hospital discharge. However, long-term safety data, especially regarding late adverse events (e.g., Clostridioides difficile infection, microbiome disruption) and the risk of fostering patient-level colonization with resistant organisms, are lacking. This gap is critical as the use of oral beta-lactams like amoxicillin-clavulanate or cephalexin expands beyond traditional oral-only pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients receiving oral beta-lactam stepdown therapy for Gram-negative bacteremia will have non-inferior rates of clinical cure and relapse at 90 days compared to a matched cohort completing intravenous therapy, but will demonstrate a higher incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events and new colonization with extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Enterobacterales within 12 months.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center, observational cohort study enrolling adult patients with uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteremia from Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, or Proteus mirabilis. The intervention cohort will be patients transitioned to an oral beta-lactam after ≥48 hours of IV therapy. A comparator cohort will be matched (1:1) on pathogen, source, and severity. Primary outcomes are clinical failure/relapse by day 90. Secondary outcomes include adverse events and resistance emergence, monitored via serial perirectal swabs at enrollment, 30 days, 90 days, and 12 months. Swabs will be cultured on selective chromogenic agar for ESBL and carbapenem-resistant organisms. Whole-genome sequencing will be performed on paired isolates to distinguish relapse from reinfection and track resistance gene acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, long-term safety and resistance profile for a high-impact oral stepdown strategy, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will generate novel data on the patient-level risk of resistance emergence following specific antibiotic exposures, a key gap in antimicrobial stewardship models. The biobank of serial isolates will enable future genomic analyses of resistance dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While stepdown therapy is studied for short-term efficacy, systematic long-term (12-month) resistance monitoring at the patient level is novel. Furthermore, focusing on oral beta-lactams for Gram-negative bacteremia moves beyond the well-studied fluoroquinolones and sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim, addressing a contemporary practice trend with unknown ecological consequences.

proposals.title: Population-Level Impact of Eravacycline Formulary Addition on Carbapenem Utilization and Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii Incidence: A Computational Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) is a critical-priority pathogen causing high-mortality infections, particularly ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). Carbapenems are a cornerstone of therapy, but their extensive use drives resistance. Eravacycline, a novel fluorocycline antibiotic with in vitro activity against CRAB, presents a potential stewardship tool, but its population-level impact on carbapenem use and resistance selection is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: The strategic introduction of eravacycline into a hospital formulary for targeted CRAB infections will significantly reduce population-level carbapenem exposure and, through this reduction, decrease the incidence of CRAB infections over a multi-year horizon.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic, compartmental transmission model of CRAB within a hospital setting, parameterized with pre-2026 clinical and microbiological data (e.g., from Ortwine's case series). The model will incorporate patient admission/transfer, infection states (susceptible, colonized, infected), and antibiotic use. We will implement a discrete-event simulation to compare two formulary strategies: a baseline (carbapenem-based) and an intervention scenario where eravacycline replaces carbapenems for a defined proportion of CRAB-VAP cases. The primary outputs will be cumulative carbapenem days of therapy and the incidence density of CRAB infections over a 5-year simulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, model-based estimate of how a novel antibiotic can be leveraged for stewardship to preserve broader antibiotic classes. It will offer hospital formularies and stewardship programs a data-driven framework for evaluating the long-term resistance impact of new antibiotic introductions, moving beyond simple efficacy and cost analyses.
proposals.novelty: While eravacycline's clinical efficacy is being studied, its role as a 'carbapenem-sparing' agent to mitigate resistance at the population level is unexplored. This proposal integrates pharmacoepidemiology, transmission dynamics, and antimicrobial stewardship into a novel modeling framework specific to CRAB.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Assess the Absence of Antiviral or Immunomodulatory Benefit from Azithromycin in Mild-to-Moderate COVID-19
proposals.motivation: Early in the COVID-19 pandemic, azithromycin was widely repurposed based on in vitro immunomodulatory and antiviral hypotheses, leading to its inclusion in many treatment protocols despite a lack of robust clinical evidence. This practice risks promoting antimicrobial resistance without clear patient benefit and obscures the true standard of care. A definitive trial to disprove its specific utility in COVID-19 is needed to guide antimicrobial stewardship and refine therapeutic guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: The addition of oral azithromycin to standard supportive care, compared to placebo, will not reduce nasopharyngeal SARS-CoV-2 viral load over time or improve clinical outcomes in patients with mild-to-moderate COVID-19, demonstrating a lack of specific antiviral or disease-modifying effect.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, double-blind, randomized controlled trial. Hospitalized and ambulatory adults with PCR-confirmed, mild-to-moderate COVID-19 will be randomized to receive either oral azithromycin (500mg day 1, then 250mg daily for 4 days) or identical placebo, in addition to standard care. The primary endpoint will be the rate of decline in nasopharyngeal SARS-CoV-2 viral load, measured by serial qRT-PCR over 10 days. Key secondary endpoints include time to clinical recovery, progression to severe disease (requiring high-flow oxygen or ICU), and changes in serum inflammatory markers (e.g., CRP, IL-6). A sample size of 400 participants will provide 90% power to detect a non-inferiority margin in viral load decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to definitively refute the hypothesized antiviral role of azithromycin in COVID-19, directly informing clinical guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship policies. It will clarify that any historical perceived benefit was likely due to confounding or treatment of bacterial co-infections, not a direct antiviral effect. The results will help conserve azithromycin for proven indications, mitigating resistance selection pressure.
proposals.novelty: While prior observational studies and some RCTs failed to show mortality benefit, this trial is specifically designed with a virologic primary endpoint to directly test and disprove the core mechanistic hypothesis of antiviral activity, rather than assessing only composite clinical outcomes. It focuses on the early disease phase where an immunomodulatory effect would be most relevant.

proposals.title: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study to Validate the Low Incidence of Bacterial Coinfection in Patients Hospitalized with Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Prior single-center studies, including our own, suggest a low incidence of bacterial co-infection in patients with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), challenging the routine use of broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics. This is critical in the pre-2026 context of rising antimicrobial resistance and the need for antibiotic stewardship. Confirming this finding in a broader population is essential to safely guide de-escalation of therapy and reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: The incidence of culture-confirmed bacterial co-infection in patients hospitalized with CAP is low (<10%) and consistent across diverse healthcare settings and patient demographics, supporting the feasibility of rapid diagnostic-guided antibiotic de-escalation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study across 5-7 geographically diverse safety-net and community hospitals. Using standardized electronic health record queries, we will identify all adult patients hospitalized with a primary diagnosis of CAP (ICD-10 codes) from January 2020 to December 2024. The primary outcome is the proportion with a positive bacterial respiratory culture (sputum/BAL) or blood culture within 48 hours of admission. We will use multivariable logistic regression to assess predictors of true bacterial co-infection, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, severity scores (e.g., CURB-65), and prior antibiotic exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, generalizable evidence on the true burden of bacterial co-infection in CAP, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines. It will establish a benchmark rate against which rapid molecular diagnostic tests can be evaluated and support the development of risk-stratified algorithms for empiric antibiotic initiation.
proposals.novelty: While single-center data exists, this will be the first large-scale study specifically designed to validate the low co-infection rate across diverse, real-world hospital settings, including safety-net hospitals that care for vulnerable populations often underrepresented in clinical trials.

proposals.title: Derivation and Validation of a Clinical-Biomarker Model to Exclude Bacterial Coinfection in Hospitalized COVID-19 Patients
proposals.motivation: During the COVID-19 pandemic, differentiating viral pneumonia from bacterial coinfection has been a major clinical challenge, leading to widespread, often unnecessary, empiric antibiotic use. This practice fuels antibiotic resistance and adverse drug events. A validated tool to reliably rule out bacterial coinfection is urgently needed to support antibiotic stewardship, especially in safety-net hospital settings with high rates of drug-resistant pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite model integrating specific clinical parameters, routine inflammatory biomarkers (e.g., procalcitonin, C-reactive protein), and novel host-response markers (e.g., soluble triggering receptor expressed on myeloid cells-1 [sTREM-1]) can achieve a high negative predictive value (>95%) for excluding bacterial coinfection in patients hospitalized with COVID-19 pneumonia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study at a safety-net hospital. Adult patients hospitalized with confirmed COVID-19 pneumonia will be enrolled. Comprehensive data including demographics, comorbidities, vital signs, and standard laboratory tests will be collected. Serum will be banked for batch analysis of procalcitonin, CRP, and sTREM-1. The reference standard for bacterial coinfection will be adjudicated by an independent expert panel using microbiological, radiological, and clinical criteria. We will use logistic regression with LASSO regularization for variable selection to derive a parsimonious prediction model, followed by internal validation via bootstrapping.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, pragmatic clinical decision rule to safely reduce unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions in COVID-19 patients. It will advance the field of antimicrobial stewardship in viral pandemics by moving beyond single biomarkers to integrated models. The findings will be directly applicable to resource-limited settings grappling with high antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined single biomarkers like procalcitonin, this proposal integrates a novel host-response marker (sTREM-1) with a robust clinical dataset specifically in a high-risk, safety-net population to create a validated rule-out model, addressing a critical gap in pandemic-era stewardship.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial of EHR-Based Behavioral Nudges to Reduce Empiric Antibiotic Use in Hospitalized Patients with COVID-19
proposals.motivation: Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, high rates of empiric antibiotic prescribing for hospitalized patients have been documented, despite low rates of confirmed bacterial co-infection. This contributes to the ongoing crisis of antimicrobial resistance and unnecessary patient harm. Behavioral science-informed interventions, such as nudges within the electronic health record (EHR), have shown promise in outpatient antibiotic stewardship but are understudied in the acute inpatient setting for viral illnesses.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a suite of EHR-based behavioral nudges, including accountable justification and suggested alternatives, will significantly reduce the rate of empiric antibiotic initiation in hospitalized adults with COVID-19 without worsening clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial across multiple hospitals within a safety-net system. The intervention involves modifying the EHR order entry for antibiotics. When a clinician initiates an antibiotic for a patient with confirmed COVID-19, a best practice advisory (BPA) will fire, requiring a mandatory free-text justification for bacterial co-infection suspicion. Concurrently, the order set will default to 'no antibiotic' and prominently display institutional guidelines recommending against routine empiric therapy, with one-click options to order procalcitonin or inflammatory markers instead. The primary outcome is the proportion of COVID-19 patients receiving antibiotics within the first 48 hours of admission.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the effectiveness of low-cost, scalable EHR nudges in improving antibiotic use during a viral pandemic. It will generate a replicable framework for integrating behavioral economics into inpatient stewardship programs. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines and EHR design to combat antibiotic overuse.
proposals.novelty: While nudges have been applied to outpatient antibiotic stewardship, this trial specifically adapts and rigorously tests them for the complex, high-stakes context of empiric decision-making in hospitalized COVID-19 patients. It combines the accountable justification nudge with a default change and diagnostic suggestion, a multi-faceted approach not previously evaluated for this specific clinical dilemma.

proposals.title: Validation of Influenza-Derived Bacterial Coinfection Diagnostic Criteria in a COVID-19 Hospitalized Patient Cohort: A Retrospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Bacterial coinfections in COVID-19 patients are a major driver of poor outcomes and inappropriate antibiotic use, yet standardized diagnostic criteria are lacking. Pre-2026, well-defined clinical, laboratory, and radiographic criteria for bacterial coinfection were developed and validated in influenza studies. Applying these established criteria to COVID-19 could improve diagnostic accuracy, guide targeted antibiotic therapy, and help combat antimicrobial resistance in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: Diagnostic criteria for bacterial coinfection (e.g., specific thresholds for procalcitonin, C-reactive protein, radiographic progression, and clinical signs) validated in influenza patients will demonstrate significant sensitivity and specificity for identifying confirmed bacterial coinfections in hospitalized COVID-19 patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients hospitalized with PCR-confirmed COVID-19 at a safety-net hospital (pre-2026 data). We will apply a composite diagnostic algorithm derived from influenza literature, incorporating timing of fever, leukocyte count, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, CRP >50 mg/L, and new/worsening pulmonary infiltrates. This algorithm's performance will be validated against a microbiological reference standard (positive respiratory or blood culture within 72h of admission). Statistical analysis will include calculation of sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values, and area under the ROC curve.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validation of a standardized, influenza-derived diagnostic framework for bacterial coinfection in COVID-19. It will offer clinicians a practical tool to better distinguish viral from bacterial pneumonia, potentially reducing unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure. The findings will directly inform future prospective studies on antibiotic stewardship in viral pandemics.
proposals.novelty: While bacterial coinfection in COVID-19 is recognized, this study systematically operationalizes and tests diagnostic criteria transferred from a different viral respiratory pathogen, rather than proposing new, untested biomarkers. It leverages pre-existing, clinically pragmatic definitions in a novel patient population with high rates of antimicrobial resistance.

proposals.title: Association of Empiric Gram-Negative Antibiotic Therapy with Vancomycin-Associated Acute Kidney Injury: A Propensity Score-Weighted Analysis to Distinguish Direct Toxicity from Diagnostic Confounding
proposals.motivation: The combination of vancomycin with anti-pseudomonal beta-lactams (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) is a cornerstone of empiric therapy for suspected hospital-acquired infections. Observational studies consistently report a higher incidence of acute kidney injury (VAKI) with this combination compared to vancomycin alone or with other agents. However, it remains unclear if this association represents a true nephrotoxic synergy or is a confounded signal, as patients receiving broad Gram-negative coverage are often sicker, have more comorbidities, or have undiagnosed conditions predisposing to AKI.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed increased risk of VAKI with combination vancomycin and anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam therapy is primarily a marker of greater baseline illness severity and misclassification of non-infectious AKI causes, rather than a direct causal nephrotoxic effect of the antibiotic combination itself.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients receiving vancomycin for ≥48 hours at a large safety-net hospital (pre-2026 data). The exposure will be concomitant empiric Gram-negative coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem). The primary outcome is VAKI (KDIGO criteria). We will use an advanced causal inference approach: 1) High-dimensional propensity score (hdPS) modeling incorporating demographics, comorbidities, vital signs, laboratory values, concomitant nephrotoxins, and source of infection to balance groups. 2) Inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) to create a pseudo-population where exposure groups are balanced on all measured confounders. 3) Compare AKI incidence between weighted groups using multivariable logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more rigorous, confounder-adjusted estimate of the independent nephrotoxicity risk of adding Gram-negative coverage to vancomycin. It will inform antimicrobial stewardship by clarifying whether avoidance of specific combinations is clinically warranted for kidney protection or if efforts should focus more on patient-level risk factors and diagnostic accuracy.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have noted the association, this analysis moves beyond standard regression by applying robust hdPS-IPTW methods specifically to disentangle drug effect from the substantial confounding by indication and severity inherent in empiric broad-spectrum therapy decisions.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Vancomycin AUC-Guided Dosing with Real-Time Cystatin C Monitoring to Prevent Vancomycin-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in High-Risk Hospitalized Patients
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin-associated acute kidney injury (V-AKI) remains a significant clinical problem, particularly in patients with high-risk features such as concomitant piperacillin-tazobactam use, pre-existing renal impairment, or critical illness. Prior to 2026, while therapeutic drug monitoring using area-under-the-curve (AUC) has been adopted to reduce nephrotoxicity, its efficacy in specifically defined high-risk populations is not well-established, and dynamic biomarkers for early AKI detection are underutilized in clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: In hospitalized patients with two or more high-risk features for V-AKI, a bundled intervention of vancomycin AUC-guided dosing (target 400-600 mg*h/L) combined with twice-weekly serum cystatin C monitoring will reduce the incidence of V-AKI by 40% compared to standard trough-guided dosing with creatinine monitoring alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial. High-risk patients (e.g., receiving concurrent nephrotoxins, ICU admission, age >65 with baseline eGFR <60 mL/min) initiated on vancomycin will be randomized 1:1 to the intervention or standard care arm. The intervention arm will use Bayesian software (e.g., MwPharm++, DoseMe) for AUC estimation from two serum levels to guide dosing, and serum cystatin C will be measured at baseline and on days 3 and 5. The primary outcome is incidence of V-AKI (KDIGO criteria) within 14 days. Secondary outcomes include time to AKI, length of stay, and rates of target AUC attainment. Statistical analysis will use intention-to-treat with logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence on the effectiveness of a precision dosing and monitoring bundle in the most vulnerable patients, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship protocols. It will also generate practical data on the utility of cystatin C as a more sensitive, real-time biomarker for AKI detection in the context of vancomycin therapy, potentially changing monitoring standards.
proposals.novelty: While AUC-guided dosing is known, its targeted application in a prospectively defined, bundled protocol for a pre-specified high-risk cohort is novel. Combining AUC targets with proactive cystatin C monitoring for early intervention represents an integrated, biomarker-driven strategy not yet evaluated in a randomized trial setting.

proposals.title: A Stewardship Intervention to Reduce Unnecessary Vancomycin Use in Acute Bacterial Skin and Skin Structure Infections: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Vancomycin is frequently overprescribed for ABSSSI in both inpatient and emergency department settings, often for non-purulent cellulitis where beta-lactams are guideline-recommended first-line therapy. This overuse drives unnecessary nephrotoxicity, increases hospital length of stay, and exacerbates the selection for vancomycin-resistant organisms, a critical threat in antimicrobial resistance. Despite stewardship programs targeting resistant pathogens, the inverse problem of de-escalating from broad-spectrum to narrower agents for common infections remains a significant, modifiable gap in care quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a multi-faceted antimicrobial stewardship intervention, including a validated clinical decision support algorithm and prospective audit with feedback, will significantly reduce the rate of unnecessary empiric vancomycin use for ABSSSI without compromising clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series study at a large safety-net hospital. The intervention comprises: 1) an EHR-embedded clinical algorithm using structured data (presentation, vital signs, leukocytosis, prior MRSA history) to flag low-risk cellulitis cases for which vancomycin is not indicated; 2) prospective audit of all empiric vancomycin starts for ABSSSI by an infectious diseases pharmacist with real-time feedback to prescribers; and 3) clinician education. The primary outcome is the rate of unnecessary empiric vancomycin days of therapy per 1000 patient-days in the 12 months post-intervention compared to the 12-month baseline, analyzed using segmented regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a practical, algorithm-supported stewardship model specifically targeting the overuse of vancomycin, a high-priority antibiotic. It will generate evidence on the feasibility and impact of de-escalation-focused interventions in ABSSSI management, directly addressing a key action point of the U.S. National Action Plan for Combating Antibiotic-Resistant Bacteria. Findings will offer a scalable template for other institutions to reduce avoidable antibiotic-related harm and resistance pressure.
proposals.novelty: While stewardship often focuses on carbapenem-resistant or multidrug-resistant organisms, this study targets the prevalent but under-addressed problem of vancomycin overuse for a common infection. It integrates a specific clinical decision algorithm for ABSSSI de-escalation into real-time stewardship workflow, moving beyond retrospective review to proactive, point-of-care intervention.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial of 7-Day vs. 14-Day Eravacycline Combination Therapy for Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) infections, particularly ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), pose a severe threat in critical care, with high mortality and limited therapeutic options. Eravacycline, a novel fluorocycline, shows in vitro activity against CRAB and is used in combination regimens, but optimal treatment duration is undefined. Prolonged antibiotic courses drive collateral damage, including microbiome disruption, Clostridioides difficile infection, and further antimicrobial resistance, creating an urgent need to define the shortest effective duration.
proposals.hypothesis: A 7-day course of intravenous eravacycline combination therapy is non-inferior to a 14-day course for the treatment of CRAB pneumonia in terms of clinical cure at test-of-cure, while reducing the incidence of adverse events associated with prolonged antibiotic exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, non-inferiority trial. Hospitalized adults with confirmed CRAB VAP or hospital-acquired pneumonia will be randomized 1:1 to receive 7 or 14 days of IV eravacycline (1.5 mg/kg q24h) combined with a second agent (e.g., sulbactam or high-dose ampicillin-sulbactam per local susceptibility). The primary endpoint is clinical cure at Day 28 after therapy end. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be used. Secondary endpoints include 28-day all-cause mortality, microbiological eradication, recurrence, and composite safety outcomes (including C. difficile infection and emergence of new resistance).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence to guide duration of eravacycline-based therapy for CRAB pneumonia, a major unmet need. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship by potentially validating a shorter, safer treatment strategy that could reduce drug-related adverse events, length of hospital stay, and selection pressure for resistance, without compromising efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Ortwine's prior case series described eravacycline use in CRAB, no prospective trials have defined its optimal treatment duration. This study moves beyond descriptive reports to rigorously test a de-escalation strategy (shorter duration) for a novel antibiotic in a resistant pathogen, a critical step not yet addressed in the literature.

proposals.title: Defining Lung Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Targets for Eravacycline in Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia: A Translational Study
proposals.motivation: Eravacycline is a novel fluorocycline antibiotic with potent in vitro activity against multidrug-resistant pathogens, including carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB), a major cause of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). However, its clinical efficacy in pneumonia is not well-defined, and optimal dosing for lung infections remains unclear. Establishing robust pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) targets in lung tissue is critical to guide effective dosing regimens, prevent treatment failure, and combat antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that achieving a specific free-drug area under the concentration-time curve to minimum inhibitory concentration (fAUC/MIC) ratio for eravacycline in epithelial lining fluid (ELF) is predictive of microbiological and clinical efficacy in patients with hospital-acquired bacterial pneumonia (HABP) and VAP.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational pharmacokinetic study in hospitalized patients with HABP/VAP receiving intravenous eravacycline. Serial plasma and bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) samples will be collected to measure total and free drug concentrations in plasma and ELF. Population PK modeling (using NONMEM) will characterize drug penetration into the lung and identify sources of inter-patient variability. PK/PD analysis will link ELF exposure metrics (fAUC/MIC, fCmax/MIC) to clinical and microbiological outcomes using logistic regression and classification and regression tree (CART) analysis to define exposure targets.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinically-derived PK/PD targets for eravacycline efficacy in lung tissue, directly informing optimal dosing strategies for pneumonia. The results will offer a rational framework for future clinical trials and stewardship guidelines, aiming to improve outcomes in infections caused by resistant Gram-negative pathogens. It will also enhance the translational understanding of eravacycline's tissue penetration.
proposals.novelty: While eravacycline's plasma PK is described, this study uniquely focuses on defining exposure targets at the primary site of infection (lung ELF) for pneumonia, a critical gap given the drug's use in CRAB VAP case series. The integration of population PK modeling with clinical outcome data to establish lung-specific targets is a novel and necessary step beyond standard plasma-based analyses.

proposals.title: Eravacycline Activity Against Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii in Clinically Relevant In Vitro Biofilm Models
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) is a critical-priority pathogen causing difficult-to-treat infections, particularly ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), where biofilm formation on medical devices is a key virulence factor. Eravacycline, a novel fluorocycline antibiotic, has shown in vitro and clinical promise against CRAB in planktonic states, but its efficacy against CRAB embedded in biofilms—a major contributor to treatment failure and recurrence—remains largely uncharacterized. This gap is critical to address to optimize therapy for complex CRAB infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that eravacycline will demonstrate superior anti-biofilm activity compared to standard-of-care antibiotics (e.g., colistin, tigecycline) against CRAB clinical isolates in vitro, and that this activity is concentration- and time-dependent.
proposals.method: We will establish static (96-well microtiter plate) and dynamic (CDC biofilm reactor) in vitro biofilm models using a panel of well-characterized CRAB clinical isolates, including strains from VAP cases. Biofilms will be treated with eravacycline across a range of clinically achievable concentrations (0.125–16 mg/L) and compared to colistin and tigecycline. Primary outcomes will be quantified via crystal violet staining for biomass and colony-forming unit counts for viable bacteria. Confocal laser scanning microscopy with live/dead staining will visualize biofilm architecture and bactericidal effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative assessment of eravacycline's anti-biofilm activity against CRAB, directly informing its potential utility for biofilm-associated infections like VAP. The data will establish crucial in vitro pharmacodynamic parameters (e.g., MBEC, biofilm prevention concentration) to guide future in vivo and clinical study design. It will offer a comparative efficacy benchmark against legacy agents, aiding antimicrobial stewardship for resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While Ortwine's prior case series examined eravacycline's clinical use in VAP, this proposal systematically investigates its fundamental activity against the biofilm phenotype central to that disease's pathophysiology, a specific mechanistic question not addressed in existing clinical reports. The direct comparison of eravacycline to other anti-CRAB agents in dynamic biofilm models moves beyond standard susceptibility testing.

proposals.title: A Multi-Center Registry Study of Eravacycline for the Treatment of Carbapenem-Resistant Acinetobacter baumannii Infections: Real-World Effectiveness and Safety
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB) is a critical-priority pathogen causing high-mortality infections, particularly in critically ill and immunocompromised patients. Eravacycline, a novel fluorocycline antibiotic, shows in vitro activity against CRAB, but real-world clinical data on its effectiveness and safety, especially in complex patient populations, are limited to small case series and retrospective studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-center registry will demonstrate that eravacycline-based regimens are associated with clinical and microbiological success in a significant proportion of patients with CRAB infections, with a definable safety profile, across diverse hospital settings.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, observational, multi-center registry across 15-20 US hospitals, including safety-net and tertiary care centers. A standardized electronic case report form will capture de-identified patient data on demographics, infection source, microbiology, eravacycline dosing and combination therapy, clinical outcomes (e.g., 30-day mortality, clinical cure), microbiological outcomes, and adverse events. Data will be analyzed using multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of treatment success and Cox proportional hazards models for time-to-event analyses, adjusting for confounders like severity of illness and concomitant infections.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the largest real-world evidence dataset on eravacycline for CRAB infections, providing crucial effectiveness and safety data to guide clinical use. It will inform optimal dosing strategies, identify patient subgroups most likely to benefit, and establish a framework for post-marketing surveillance of novel antibiotics against drug-resistant pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Ortwine et al. reported a single-center case series, this proposal scales to a structured, multi-center registry designed specifically for eravacycline in CRAB, enabling robust comparative effectiveness research and generalizable conclusions not possible from isolated reports.

proposals.title: Inferring Genetic Determinants of Eravacycline Resistance from Clinical Failure Cases Using Whole-Genome Sequencing and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Eravacycline is a critical fluorocycline antibiotic for treating infections caused by multidrug-resistant Gram-negative pathogens, including carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii (CRAB). However, as its clinical use expands, particularly in vulnerable populations like COVID-19 patients, the emergence of resistance threatens its long-term utility. Current surveillance identifies resistance after it is established; a method to rapidly predict genetic markers from early clinical failure data is urgently needed to guide stewardship and preserve efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying machine learning to whole-genome sequences and clinical metadata from patients with eravacycline treatment failure, we can identify novel, non-canonical genetic variants and gene combinations predictive of emerging resistance.
proposals.method: We will perform whole-genome sequencing on bacterial isolates from a retrospective cohort of patients with documented eravacycline treatment failure (e.g., from the VAP case series) and matched clinical success controls. We will construct a feature set encompassing single nucleotide variants, insertions/deletions, and plasmid/gene presence-absence from assembled genomes. Using a Random Forest classifier and gradient boosting (XGBoost), we will model the relationship between genomic features and the binary outcome of treatment failure/success, controlling for clinical confounders. Feature importance analysis (e.g., SHAP values) will prioritize candidate resistance markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a predictive model linking genomic signatures to eravacycline clinical failure, providing a tool for early detection of resistance. It will also identify putative novel resistance mechanisms beyond the known ribosomal protection and efflux pumps, informing basic science and diagnostic development. The 'inverse problem' framework can be adapted for other novel antibiotics.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the typical resistance discovery pipeline, starting from clinical outcome rather than in vitro susceptibility testing, to uncover markers directly relevant to therapeutic failure. Integrating machine learning with WGS of failure cases is a proactive strategy not yet widely applied to eravacycline.

proposals.title: A Clinical Decision Support System to Optimize Fluoroquinolone Prescribing: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Fluoroquinolone overuse drives resistance, but underuse in appropriate scenarios (e.g., specific Gram-negative infections, penicillin-allergic patients) can lead to worse outcomes. Current antimicrobial stewardship programs often focus on restriction, which may inadvertently suppress necessary use. This creates a need for interventions that actively promote optimal, guideline-concordant fluoroquinolone therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a patient-specific, electronic health record (EHR)-embedded clinical decision support system (CDSS) that flags scenarios where fluoroquinolones are guideline-recommended will increase their appropriate use without increasing overall prescription volume.
proposals.method: We will develop a rules-based CDSS algorithm using pre-2026 IDSA/ATS guidelines, integrated into the Epic EHR. It will analyze patient data (allergies, culture/susceptibility, infection source, renal function) to generate real-time, interruptive alerts recommending fluoroquinolone initiation or oral stepdown when criteria are met. The intervention will be evaluated using a stepped-wedge cluster randomized design across hospitalist services, with the primary outcome being the rate of guideline-concordant fluoroquinolone therapy, assessed via manual chart review.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a scalable model for 'pro-stewardship' that promotes optimal antibiotic selection rather than just restriction. It will generate evidence on whether nudging towards appropriate use can be decoupled from overall consumption, a key challenge in stewardship. The CDSS framework can be adapted for other antibiotic classes.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical stewardship paradigm from blocking 'bad' use to actively prompting 'good' use for fluoroquinolones. It moves beyond static guidelines by implementing a dynamic, patient-specific algorithm within clinician workflow, a method not widely tested for this specific purpose pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Multimodal Stewardship Bundle to Reduce Inappropriate Outpatient Azithromycin Prescribing in a Safety-Net Health System: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Azithromycin is one of the most commonly prescribed antibiotics in outpatient settings, with high rates of use for conditions like acute bronchitis where it is rarely indicated, driving resistance and adverse events. Prior successful antibiotic stewardship bundles have focused on inpatient or broad-spectrum agents, leaving a significant gap in scalable interventions for high-volume, community-targeted antibiotics. This is particularly critical in safety-net hospitals where resource constraints and high patient acuity may influence prescribing patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a multimodal stewardship intervention (clinician education, EHR-based clinical decision support alerts, and standardized order sets) will significantly reduce the rate of inappropriate azithromycin prescriptions for adult outpatients with acute respiratory infections, without increasing clinic revisit rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series study at a large urban safety-net hospital and its affiliated clinics. The intervention bundle includes: 1) a targeted education module for primary care providers on guideline-concordant management of acute bronchitis and sinusitis; 2) a best practice advisory (BPA) in the EHR firing for azithromycin orders for these diagnoses, suggesting alternatives and requiring a justification; and 3) integrated order sets promoting symptomatic therapies. The primary outcome is the monthly proportion of inappropriate azithromycin prescriptions for target diagnoses, analyzed using segmented regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for the effectiveness of a scalable, EHR-embedded stewardship strategy specifically for a high-use outpatient antibiotic, extending the bundle concept beyond traditional inpatient targets. It will generate a practical model for health systems to curb overuse of macrolides, potentially reducing selective pressure for resistance in community pathogens like Streptococcus pneumoniae.
proposals.novelty: While bundles have been applied to fluoroquinolones or broad-spectrum agents, a focused intervention on azithromycin in the outpatient safety-net setting is novel. It adapts a proven multimodal framework to a distinct prescribing context where habitual use and patient expectations are major drivers, testing its generalizability to a different antibiotic class and care environment.

proposals.title: Oral-Only Versus Intravenous-to-Oral Stepdown Therapy for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Randomized, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a major cause of morbidity and mortality, traditionally requiring initial intravenous (IV) antibiotic therapy. This necessitates hospital admission or prolonged IV access, increasing healthcare costs, patient discomfort, and risk of IV-line complications. In the pre-2026 era, with rising antimicrobial resistance and pressure on hospital resources, there is a critical need to re-evaluate the necessity of IV therapy for low-risk, uncomplicated infections, especially given the high bioavailability of modern oral antibiotics like fluoroquinolones and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
proposals.hypothesis: For carefully selected patients with uncomplicated GNBSI caused by susceptible pathogens, initial therapy with highly bioavailable oral antibiotics is non-inferior to standard initial IV therapy followed by an oral stepdown in terms of clinical cure at 30 days.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial at a safety-net hospital. Adults with uncomplicated GNBSI (defined by source control, absence of severe sepsis/immunosuppression, and susceptible pathogen) will be randomized 1:1 to either oral-only therapy (e.g., ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, or TMP-SMX based on susceptibility) or standard IV-to-oral stepdown therapy. The primary outcome is clinical cure at 30-day follow-up. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be used. Secondary outcomes include time to defervescence, length of stay, adverse events, and 90-day recurrence. Statistical analysis will be by intention-to-treat and per-protocol.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to challenge the dogma of mandatory initial IV therapy for uncomplicated GNBSI. It could establish a new, patient-centric treatment paradigm that reduces hospitalizations, healthcare costs, and complications associated with IV access. The results will directly inform clinical practice guidelines for the management of bloodstream infections.
proposals.novelty: While stepdown studies exist, this proposal directly challenges the initial IV phase itself for a defined GNBSI population, moving beyond streptococcal infections to the more common and clinically impactful gram-negative pathogens. It tests a true oral-first strategy from the outset, which is a significant paradigm shift from current standard of care.

proposals.title: A Non-Inferiority Trial of a 5-Day vs 7-Day Course of Antibiotics for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A PK/PD-Modeled Approach Inspired by Radiation Fractionation
proposals.motivation: Standard 7-14 day antibiotic courses for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are based on convention, not robust evidence, and contribute to antimicrobial resistance, adverse drug events, and prolonged hospitalization. The success of hypofractionated radiation therapy, which challenged similar dogmas using biological dose modeling and precise patient selection, provides a powerful methodological analogy for optimizing antibiotic duration. This approach is urgently needed to define the minimal effective therapy for uncomplicated GNBSI, a common and costly infection.
proposals.hypothesis: For a precisely defined population with uncomplicated GNBSI, a 5-day antibiotic course, designed using pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) principles to deliver a 'biologically effective dose' equivalent to a standard 7-day course, will be non-inferior in terms of clinical cure.
proposals.method: First, we will develop a PK/PD model using Monte Carlo simulation (e.g., in NONMEM) for common Gram-negative pathogens and antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone, cefepime, meropenem) to identify a 5-day dosing regimen predicted to achieve a time above MIC or fAUC/MIC target equivalent to 7 days. This model will inform the intervention arm of a prospective, randomized, controlled, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Eligible adults with uncomplicated GNBSI (defined by source control, defervescence within 72h, and non-immunocompromised status) will be randomized to the model-optimized 5-day or standard 7-day course. The primary endpoint is clinical cure at a 14-day test-of-cure visit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level evidence to challenge the default 7-day duration for uncomplicated GNBSI, potentially establishing a new standard of care that reduces antibiotic exposure. It will demonstrate the utility of formal PK/PD modeling, analogous to biological dose modeling in radiation oncology, to rationally design shorter-course antibiotic trials. The findings could directly inform antimicrobial stewardship guidelines and reduce the burden of resistance.
proposals.novelty: The novel conceptual framework adapts the paradigm-shifting methodology of radiation fractionation trials—using biological equivalence modeling and strict patient stratification—to the field of infectious diseases, moving beyond simple duration comparisons. It applies sophisticated PK/PD simulation not just for dose justification but explicitly to design a shorter, biologically equivalent treatment course for a trial.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled, Non-Inferiority Trial of 5-Day versus 7-Day Antibiotic Therapy for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections (SHORT-GN Trial)
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a major cause of morbidity and mortality, and prolonged antibiotic courses are a key driver of antimicrobial resistance, Clostridioides difficile infection, and adverse drug events. The traditional 7-14 day treatment duration is based on limited evidence, and recent pre-2026 studies in other infections (e.g., pneumonia, intra-abdominal) support shorter courses. Rigorously testing shorter durations for uncomplicated GNBSI is critical to optimize therapy and combat resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A 5-day total antibiotic course is non-inferior to a 7-day course for the treatment of uncomplicated GNBSI, as measured by the composite endpoint of clinical cure and absence of microbiological relapse within 30 days.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled non-inferiority trial. Adults hospitalized with uncomplicated GNBSI (defined by source control, defervescence within 72h, and non-persistent bacteremia) will be randomized after 5 days of effective intravenous therapy to either 2 additional days of active drug (7-day arm) or 2 days of matching placebo (5-day arm). The primary endpoint is a composite of clinical cure and absence of microbiological relapse at 30-day follow-up. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be used, with sample size calculated accordingly. Secondary endpoints include mortality, resistance emergence, and C. difficile infection rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to challenge the dogma of prolonged therapy for GNBSI, potentially establishing a new standard of care for uncomplicated cases. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs by offering a safe, evidence-based strategy to reduce antibiotic exposure, thereby mitigating selection for resistant pathogens and reducing healthcare costs.
proposals.novelty: While shorter-course therapy has been explored for other infections, a definitive trial targeting the specific and common syndrome of uncomplicated GNBSI is lacking. This proposal rigorously defines 'uncomplicated' and uses a blinded, placebo-controlled design to isolate the effect of duration, moving beyond observational or unblinded step-down studies.

proposals.title: ADAPT-GNBSI: An Adaptive Platform Trial of Oral Step-Down Therapy for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections Using Bayesian Response-Adaptive Randomization
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a leading cause of sepsis and mortality, with rising resistance complicating intravenous therapy. While oral step-down can reduce hospital length of stay and healthcare costs, evidence is limited to specific pathogens and older antibiotics, failing to reflect the contemporary, dynamic resistance landscape. A static trial design cannot keep pace with new oral agents like cefiderocol or novel beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations entering the market.
proposals.hypothesis: A perpetual adaptive platform trial, which dynamically updates treatment arms based on interim efficacy and safety data, will efficiently identify the most effective oral step-down regimens for uncomplicated GNBSI and maintain relevance as resistance patterns and therapeutic options evolve.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized, open-label platform trial using a master protocol with a shared control arm of continued IV therapy. Initial experimental arms will include oral fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and newer agents like oral fosfomycin. A Bayesian response-adaptive randomization algorithm (e.g., Thompson sampling) will update allocation probabilities every 100 patients, favoring regimens with higher posterior probability of success (primary outcome: clinical cure at 30 days). Pre-specified futility and superiority boundaries will trigger dropping or concluding superiority of arms. A protocol committee will review and integrate new oral regimens as they receive regulatory approval.
proposals.contribution: This will create a continuously updated, high-grade evidence base for oral step-down in GNBSI, directly informing clinical guidelines with contemporary data. The adaptive design will efficiently allocate patients to more effective therapies within the trial and establish a methodological framework for perpetual evaluation of infectious disease therapeutics in an era of rapid antimicrobial development and resistance.
proposals.novelty: This applies the adaptive platform trial model, proven in oncology, to the dynamic problem of antimicrobial therapy for GNBSI, creating a 'living' trial that evolves with the antibiotic market. It moves beyond single-regimen comparisons to a system that perpetually identifies optimal oral step-down strategies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Domain Machine Learning Model to Predict Optimal Antibiotic Duration in Hospitalized Patients with Community-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic duration is a critical determinant of patient outcomes and antimicrobial resistance, yet current guidelines rely on pathogen-class averages or clinical trial data that ignore patient heterogeneity. This one-size-fits-all approach leads to both treatment failure from under-treatment and collateral damage from over-treatment, fueling resistance. A precision framework integrating pathogen, host, and drug-specific data is urgently needed to tailor therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite signature derived from bacterial genomic features, host inflammatory markers (e.g., PCT, IL-6), and site-of-infection pharmacokinetics will more accurately predict the time to sustained clinical and microbiological resolution than pathogen identity or clinical severity scores alone, enabling shorter, effective courses for many patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of 300 hospitalized adults with community-acquired pneumonia. We will collect serial bacterial isolates (for whole-genome sequencing and transcriptomic profiling), plasma/sputum for host immune markers, and drug concentration measurements. A gradient boosting machine (XGBoost) model will be trained to predict a composite endpoint of sustained resolution, using features from these domains alongside clinical data. The model will be validated temporally and its output used to simulate duration recommendations against a 7-day standard.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, data-driven algorithm for personalized antibiotic duration, moving beyond fixed-duration rules. It will provide proof-of-concept that integrating multi-domain signatures can safely shorten therapy, directly addressing the dual challenges of treatment failure and antimicrobial resistance in a high-burden infection.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on pathogen resistance or host biomarkers in isolation, this model dynamically integrates real-time bacterial behavior (transcriptomics), host response, and drug exposure at the infection site to define a patient-specific 'resolution signature', a novel approach to duration optimization.

proposals.title: Pathogen-Specific Pharmacodynamic Targets for Treatment Duration: A PK/PD Framework to Optimize Antibiotic Therapy for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Current antibiotic treatment durations are largely empiric, based on clinical trial data for heterogeneous populations. This one-size-fits-all approach may lead to unnecessary prolonged therapy for some pathogens, increasing toxicity and resistance pressure, while being insufficient for others, risking relapse. In the pre-2026 context of rising antimicrobial resistance and a push for antibiotic stewardship, a more precise, pathogen-driven framework is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pharmacodynamic exposure required for durable bacterial eradication, and thus the optimal treatment duration, varies significantly between key Gram-negative pathogens (*Escherichia coli*, *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*), and that these differences can be quantified using established PK/PD indices (e.g., AUC/MIC, %T>MIC) in predictive models.
proposals.method: We will employ a translational approach. First, in vitro hollow-fiber infection models (HFIM) will be used to simulate human pharmacokinetics of standard antibiotics (e.g., meropenem, cefepime) against defined strains. We will identify the PK/PD index thresholds (e.g., time above a multiple of the MIC) associated with sustained eradication and suppression of resistance. Second, these targets will be validated in a murine thigh infection model, correlating achieved PK/PD exposure with microbiologic cure at different simulated treatment durations (e.g., 5, 7, 10 days). Population pharmacokinetic modeling will be used to translate these targets into clinical dosing and duration guidance.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first pathogen-specific PK/PD targets for treatment cessation, moving beyond fixed durations. It will provide a mechanistic, data-driven framework for designing future clinical trials of shorter, optimized therapy. Ultimately, it aims to reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure and slow resistance emergence while maintaining clinical efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD indices are standard for dose *optimization*, their direct application to define pathogen-specific treatment *duration* is novel. This adapts a core pharmacological principle from the dosing problem to the duration problem, a critical but understudied aspect of antimicrobial therapy.

proposals.title: Pathogen-Specific Optimization of Antibiotic Duration for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Comparative Effectiveness Study
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GN-BSI) often recommend a uniform ~7-day antibiotic course. However, this one-size-fits-all approach may be suboptimal, as pathogens like Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa have distinct virulence and resistance profiles. In an era of rising antimicrobial resistance and emphasis on antibiotic stewardship, refining duration guidance is critical to minimize unnecessary antibiotic exposure without compromising clinical outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal duration of antibiotic therapy for GN-BSI varies by causative pathogen, with some requiring shorter courses (e.g., 5-7 days for E. coli) and others requiring longer courses (e.g., >7 days for P. aeruginosa) to achieve equivalent clinical cure and prevent relapse.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with uncomplicated GN-BSI from 2020-2024. Using electronic health record data, we will compare clinical outcomes (30-day relapse, all-cause mortality) between patients receiving shorter (≤7 days) vs. longer (>7 days) therapy, stratified by pathogen (E. coli, K. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa).
