We will employ multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to control for confounders (source control, severity, resistance). A time-to-event analysis will model the hazard of relapse relative to treatment duration for each pathogen.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative effectiveness evidence for pathogen-specific antibiotic durations in GN-BSI, moving beyond the uniform recommendation. It will generate data to support future randomized trials and inform more precise, stewardship-focused clinical guidelines, potentially reducing aggregate antibiotic use.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared short vs. long courses for GN-BSI broadly, none have rigorously evaluated whether the optimal duration differs meaningfully between the most common Gram-negative pathogens, a key nuance for personalized therapy.

proposals.title: A Physiology-Informed, Dynamic Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Model to Personalize Oral Step-Down Therapy in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) is increasingly used to shorten hospital stays and reduce costs, but its optimization is hindered by static PK/PD models. These models fail to account for the dynamic physiological recovery of patients—such as fluctuating renal function and variable gastrointestinal absorption—during the transition from IV to oral therapy, potentially leading to suboptimal dosing, treatment failure, or toxicity. This is especially critical in the context of rising antimicrobial resistance, where precise antibiotic exposure is paramount for efficacy and resistance suppression.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating time-varying, patient-specific physiological parameters into a dynamic PK/PD model will generate more accurate predictions of oral antibiotic exposure than standard static models, enabling personalized dosing that improves clinical outcomes in GNBSI step-down therapy.
proposals.method: We will develop a mechanism-based, non-linear mixed-effects model using NONMEM 7.5. The model will integrate population PK parameters for oral fluoroquinolones (e.g., levofloxacin) and beta-lactams (e.g., oral cephalosporins) with time-courses of key covariates: estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), serum albumin, C-reactive protein (CRP), and clinical scores of GI motility. Model development and validation will use a retrospective cohort of GNBSI patients from a safety-net hospital who underwent oral step-down, with densely sampled drug levels and biomarker data. The final platform will perform Bayesian forecasting to recommend patient- and day-specific oral doses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first PK/PD modeling framework that dynamically adjusts to a patient's recovery physiology, moving beyond the 'one-size-fits-all' approach for oral step-down. It will deliver a clinically actionable tool to personalize the timing and dosing of oral antibiotics, aiming to reduce treatment failure rates and length of stay. The model structure will also establish a new paradigm for considering host recovery in antimicrobial pharmacometrics.
proposals.novelty: While population PK models exist, they treat covariates as static; our approach uniquely models the *trajectory* of physiological recovery as a driver of drug exposure. This directly addresses the pathophysiological mismatch inherent in applying drug development models, derived from stable volunteers, to acutely ill, recovering patients.

proposals.title: Defining a Clinical Injury Level: An Economic Threshold Model to Optimize Oral Step-Down Therapy for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) is increasingly used to shorten hospital stays and reduce costs, but optimal dosing and duration are poorly defined. Excessive antibiotic exposure drives resistance and adverse events, while insufficient therapy risks relapse. Current guidelines lack a quantitative framework to balance these competing risks, analogous to the challenge in agriculture of applying pesticide only when economically justified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an Economic Injury Level (EIL) model, adapted as a Clinical Injury Level (CIL), can identify a pathogen burden threshold below which oral therapy maintains clinical cure, thereby defining the minimal effective oral regimen (dose/duration) for GNBSI.
proposals.method: We will develop a pharmacodynamic model integrating in vitro kill curves for common GN pathogens (e.g., E. coli, Klebsiella) against oral agents (e.g., fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) with patient pharmacokinetic data. Using a mathematical framework mirroring agricultural EIL, we will define the CIL as the bacterial density where the 'cost' of treatment failure equals the 'cost' of extended therapy. This model will be calibrated and validated using retrospective clinical data from patients with GNBSI who received oral step-down, assessing outcomes against modeled predictions of pathogen suppression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative decision-support tool for personalizing oral step-down therapy, aiming to minimize total antibiotic exposure and reduce selection for resistance. It will offer a methodological bridge between ecological management principles and clinical infectious diseases, moving beyond fixed-duration protocols.
proposals.novelty: The direct application of agricultural economic threshold models to antibiotic dosing optimization is unprecedented. It reframes the therapeutic challenge not as total eradication, but as maintaining pathogen burden below a clinically defined injury threshold, a paradigm shift from current practice.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Pediatric Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Model to Guide Oral Step-Down Therapy for Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) in children are increasingly complicated by antimicrobial resistance, necessitating prolonged intravenous therapy and hospitalization. While oral step-down therapy is a cornerstone of antimicrobial stewardship in adults, its application in pediatrics is hindered by a lack of age-specific pharmacological data and validated criteria for defining 'uncomplicated' infections. This gap forces extended hospital stays, increasing costs and patient burden, without evidence-based guidance on safe and effective oral transition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pediatric physiologically-based pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PBPK/PD) models can accurately simulate drug exposure for key oral antibiotics (e.g., fluoroquinolones, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) across pediatric age groups and identify PK/PD targets (e.g., fAUC/MIC) predictive of microbiological cure for uncomplicated GNBSI.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate pediatric PBPK models for 2-3 oral antibiotics using established software (e.g., Simcyp Pediatric, GastroPlus). Models will incorporate age-dependent physiological parameters (organ weights, blood flows, enzyme maturation). These will be linked to a PD module for common GNBSI pathogens (E. coli, Klebsiella spp.) using pre-2026 MIC distributions. Monte Carlo simulations (n=10,000) across virtual pediatric populations (neonate to adolescent) will be performed to calculate the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens and MICs. The primary outcome is the identification of dosing regimens achieving ≥90% PTA for a predefined PK/PD target.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first pharmacological framework for defining pediatric-specific eligibility for oral step-down in GNBSI, moving beyond extrapolated adult criteria. It will generate optimized, age-stratified oral dosing regimens, directly supporting clinical guideline development. The project will deliver a validated PBPK/PD simulation tool for future evaluation of new oral agents against resistant pediatric pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While PBPK modeling is established in drug development, its targeted application to define clinical treatment pathways for pediatric bloodstream infections is novel. This approach uniquely integrates pathogen-specific PD, real-world MIC data, and the full spectrum of pediatric physiology to solve a specific, high-impact clinical stewardship problem.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Modeling to Optimize Oral Step-Down Therapy for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Prospective, Observational Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Oral step-down therapy for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) is increasingly considered to reduce hospital length of stay and healthcare costs, but its application is hindered by concerns over inadequate drug exposure and the rise of resistant pathogens. Current dosing relies on fixed regimens and susceptibility breakpoints, which may not account for inter-patient variability in drug absorption and clearance, potentially leading to suboptimal outcomes. A PK/PD modeling approach, standard in drug development, is needed to rationally define optimal oral dosing strategies for pathogens like Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae in a real-world patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patient-specific Monte Carlo simulation models, integrating oral bioavailability and pathogen MIC distributions, will identify oral fluoroquinolone and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole dosing regimens that achieve probability of target attainment (PTA) ≥90% for GNBSI, and that these model-optimized regimens will be associated with non-inferior clinical success compared to standard intravenous therapy in a matched cohort.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational study of patients with GNBSI transitioning to oral step-down therapy. Serial plasma samples will be collected to measure drug concentrations of the oral agent used. Population PK models will be developed using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM 7.5). These models will be linked with local MIC distributions for target pathogens. Monte Carlo simulations (10,000 subjects) will be performed to calculate the PTA for various dosing regimens against pharmacodynamic targets (e.g., fAUC/MIC). The primary clinical analysis will compare outcomes in patients receiving model-supported regimens versus a historical cohort receiving standard IV therapy, using propensity score matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, data-driven framework for selecting and dosing oral antibiotics for GNBSI, moving beyond empirical rules. It will generate institution-specific dosing recommendations that maximize the probability of therapeutic success while considering local resistance patterns. The study will directly inform clinical guidelines on oral step-down therapy and offer a methodological template applicable to other infection types and antimicrobials.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling is established for IV antibiotics in drug development, its systematic application to optimize oral regimens for serious Gram-negative infections in a real-world clinical setting is novel. This approach moves beyond validating existing oral step-down protocols to actively designing and prospectively evaluating regimens based on a quantitative, patient- and pathogen-specific model.

proposals.title: ADAPT-GNBSI: An Adaptive Platform Trial Integrating Diagnostic and Therapeutic Strategies for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a leading cause of sepsis and mortality, complicated by rising antibiotic resistance. Optimal management requires both effective antibiotic therapy and identification of the infection source, yet evidence is lacking on the best duration of therapy and the necessity of a standardized, resource-intensive diagnostic workup for all patients. This leads to variable practice, potential overuse of antibiotics and imaging, and suboptimal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive trial design that concurrently modifies antibiotic strategy and diagnostic imaging allocation based on interim outcomes will efficiently identify patient subgroups for whom a mandatory, standardized source investigation protocol, combined with specific antibiotic regimens, yields superior clinical cure rates compared to a selective, criteria-based approach.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-arm, multi-stage (MAMS) adaptive platform trial in hospitalized adults with GNBSI. The trial will randomize patients to multiple antibiotic strategy arms (e.g., short vs. standard duration, beta-lactam monotherapy vs. combination) and two diagnostic strategy arms (mandatory standardized imaging vs. selective imaging). A Bayesian response-adaptive randomization algorithm will be used, with pre-specified interim analyses. Arms may be dropped for futility, and the randomization ratio to the diagnostic strategies will be dynamically adjusted based on accumulating data to favor the strategy showing superior benefit within pre-defined patient subgroups (e.g., by pathogen, severity, or early clinical response).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for a personalized, integrated approach to GNBSI management. It will generate actionable guidelines on antibiotic duration and the targeted use of source investigation, aiming to improve patient outcomes while promoting antimicrobial stewardship and efficient resource utilization in the hospital setting.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively combines the evaluation of a therapeutic intervention (antibiotic strategy) and a diagnostic management pathway within a single adaptive platform, allowing for dynamic, data-driven optimization of both components based on real-time trial results, which is distinct from traditional, static trial designs.

proposals.title: Validation of a Dynamic Host-Response Biomarker Algorithm to Personalize Antibiotic Duration in Pediatric Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pediatric Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a major cause of morbidity, yet optimal antibiotic duration is poorly defined, leading to prolonged therapy that drives resistance and adverse effects. Current 'uncomplicated' definitions are static checklists that fail to capture individual host-response dynamics. A biomarker-driven, personalized approach is urgently needed to safely de-escalate or shorten therapy in children.
proposals.hypothesis: A pediatric-specific algorithm integrating serial measurements of procalcitonin, C-reactive protein, interleukin-6, and neutrophil CD64 index can accurately define immunological resolution ('rapid resolvers') and, when used to guide therapy, will non-inferiorly achieve clinical cure while significantly reducing antibiotic exposure compared to standard duration based on guideline checklists.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of children with GNBSI at a tertiary pediatric hospital. Serial blood samples will be collected at diagnosis, 24-48h, 72h, and day 5-7 for a predefined biomarker panel. Using pre-2026 statistical methods (latent class analysis, group-based trajectory modeling), we will derive phenotypes of host-response resolution. A dynamic risk-stratification algorithm will be developed, defining 'uncomplicated' by biomarker normalization trajectories. Its predictive validity for safe early de-escalation will be assessed against clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, biomarker-based definition of 'uncomplicated' pediatric GNBSI, moving beyond microbiological criteria. It will deliver a concrete, dynamic decision-support algorithm for clinicians to personalize antibiotic duration, aiming to set a new standard for antimicrobial stewardship in pediatric sepsis. The resulting host-response phenotypes may also inform future trials of immunomodulatory therapies.
proposals.novelty: This study fuses the concept of antibiotic stepdown (from Ortwine's prior work) with deep host-response phenotyping specifically for pediatric GNBSI, creating a dynamic, physiology-driven management tool rather than a static checklist. It applies advanced trajectory modeling of a multi-biomarker panel to define treatment duration, a novel approach in children.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Clinical Outcomes of a Simplified, Consensus-Based Antibiotic Decision Algorithm for Community-Acquired Pneumonia in Resource-Limited Hospitals: A Pragmatic, Stepped-Wedge Cluster Trial
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a global crisis disproportionately affecting resource-limited settings (RLS), where access to advanced diagnostics and broad-spectrum antibiotics is constrained. Pragmatic treatment algorithms, like the consensus approaches studied in high-income settings for streamlining therapy, have not been validated in RLS, where they could provide crucial guidance to optimize empiric therapy and preserve last-line agents.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a simplified, consensus-based antibiotic decision algorithm, derived from local epidemiology and drug availability, for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in RLS hospitals will be feasible and will result in non-inferior clinical cure rates while reducing the use of broad-spectrum, reserve antibiotics compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across 6-8 hospitals in low- and middle-income countries. The intervention is a paper-based algorithm integrating syndromic presentation (e.g., CURB-65), locally validated risk factors for drug-resistant pathogens (from data like Ortwine's prior work), and a strict hierarchy of antibiotic choices based on availability and WHO AWaRe classification. Feasibility will be measured via algorithm adherence surveys and qualitative interviews. Effectiveness will be assessed by comparing clinical cure at day 7 and days of therapy with Watch/Reserve antibiotics between control and intervention phases, adjusting for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for the operational feasibility and clinical impact of a context-adapted stewardship algorithm in RLS. It will yield a transferable framework for developing similar tools for other syndromes, directly contributing to the global fight against AMR by promoting effective, context-appropriate antibiotic use.
proposals.novelty: While consensus algorithms exist, this study explicitly translates and tests the concept in RLS, where diagnostic and formulary constraints are fundamental design parameters, not just implementation barriers, moving beyond descriptive epidemiology to interventional implementation science.

proposals.title: Prospective Derivation and Validation of the METRIC-GN Score: A Clinical-Microbiological Risk Score for Metastatic Complications in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infection
proposals.motivation: Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) are a leading cause of sepsis and mortality, with a significant subset of patients developing metastatic complications (e.g., endocarditis, septic emboli) or relapse, which demand prolonged therapy and intensive monitoring. Current clinical decisions on treatment duration and discharge planning are based on expert opinion and heterogeneous risk factors, lacking a validated, objective tool. In the pre-2026 era of rising antimicrobial resistance and pressure for outpatient antibiotic therapy, a precise risk stratification tool is urgently needed to optimize resource use and patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multivariate model incorporating readily available clinical, microbiological, and biomarker data at the time of GNBSI diagnosis can accurately stratify patients by their risk of developing metastatic complications, relapse, or infection-related mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center observational cohort study of adults with GNBSI. Standardized data on demographics, comorbidities, quick SOFA, source control, pathogen species, genotypic resistance markers (via PCR/whole-genome sequencing), and serial procalcitonin will be collected. The primary composite outcome is metastatic complication, microbiological relapse, or 30-day infection-related mortality. Using the derivation cohort, a multivariate logistic regression model with LASSO regularization for variable selection will be developed to generate a point-based risk score. This METRIC-GN score will be internally validated via bootstrapping and externally validated in a separate cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospectively derived and validated clinical prediction rule specifically for complicated GNBSI. It will offer clinicians an evidence-based tool to identify high-risk patients requiring prolonged intravenous therapy and close monitoring, while safely enabling oral stepdown and early discharge for low-risk patients, aligning with antimicrobial stewardship goals. The integration of genotypic resistance data adds a modern precision medicine dimension to traditional clinical prediction.
proposals.novelty: While scores exist for mortality (e.g., SOFA) or source identification, no pre-2026 tool prospectively combines clinical, advanced microbiological (genotypic), and biomarker data to predict the specific outcome of metastatic complications in GNBSI, a gap critical for personalizing therapy duration.

proposals.title: A Model-Predictive Control Framework for Optimizing Antibiotic De-escalation Timing in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: De-escalation from broad- to narrow-spectrum antibiotics in Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) is a cornerstone of antimicrobial stewardship, but its optimal timing remains uncertain. Premature de-escalation risks clinical failure, while delayed de-escalation unnecessarily drives resistance. Current guidelines rely on static timepoints or culture results, lacking a dynamic, patient-specific strategy to navigate this trade-off.
proposals.hypothesis: A model-predictive control (MPC) algorithm, using serial clinical and biomarker data, can generate patient-specific, optimal de-escalation times that minimize a combined cost function of treatment failure and antibiotic spectrum exposure, outperforming standard fixed-duration strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a retrospective cohort of GNBSI patients with daily data (vitals, labs, biomarkers like procalcitonin, treatment details). A recurrent neural network (RNN) will model individual patient trajectory. An MPC algorithm, with a cost function weighting clinical deterioration risk and antibiotic spectrum, will be simulated to determine optimal de-escalation days. Performance will be compared against actual clinician decisions and a 72-hour rule using counterfactual outcome modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a computational, patient-centered framework for antibiotic de-escalation, moving beyond one-size-fits-all rules. It will quantify the theoretical trade-off between individual risk and stewardship benefit, offering a novel methodology for optimizing dynamic treatment regimens in sepsis.
proposals.novelty: It adapts a control theory paradigm (MPC) from engineering to the clinical problem of treatment switching, framing de-escalation as a predictive control optimization rather than a reactive decision based on lagging indicators.

proposals.title: A Phase III, Randomized, Double-Blind, Multicenter Trial Comparing the Efficacy and Safety of a Novel Beta-Lactam/Beta-Lactamase Inhibitor Combination versus Best Available Therapy for the Treatment of Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Caused by Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Hospital-acquired and ventilator-associated pneumonia (HAP/VAP) caused by carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical unmet medical need with high mortality rates and limited, often toxic, treatment options. The rapid global spread of carbapenemases like KPC and NDM, coupled with a dearth of robust, large-scale clinical trial data for novel agents in this specific setting, leaves clinicians with inadequate evidence to guide therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination (e.g., cefepime/taniborbactam, based on pre-2026 Phase II data) will demonstrate non-inferiority in clinical cure at Test of Cure and superiority in 28-day all-cause mortality compared to best available therapy (e.g., polymyxin- or aminoglycoside-based regimens) for the treatment of CRE HAP/VAP.
proposals.method: This will be a global, randomized, double-blind, active-controlled Phase III trial. Approximately 450 adult patients with clinically and microbiologically confirmed CRE HAP/VAP will be randomized 1:1 to receive either the investigational combination or best available therapy (BAT), with BAT selection from a pre-specified list guided by local susceptibility. The primary efficacy endpoint will be clinical cure rate at the Test of Cure visit (7-14 days after end of therapy). Key secondary endpoints include 28-day all-cause mortality, microbiological eradication, and safety/tolerability assessments. Statistical analysis will employ a non-inferiority margin of 10% for the primary endpoint, with hierarchical testing for superiority on mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from a dedicated Phase III trial for a novel beta-lactam-based agent specifically targeting CRE HAP/VAP, a leading cause of death from antimicrobial resistance. It will establish a new standard of care for this infection, moving therapy away from toxic, older agents and informing future treatment guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While recent studies, including Ortwine's work, have examined resistant pathogens in pneumonia, no large, randomized Phase III trial has prospectively focused exclusively on CRE as the causative pathogen in HAP/VAP, using a novel, targeted beta-lactamase inhibitor combination as the investigational arm.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Beta-Lactam Alternatives versus Anti-staphylococcal Penicillins for Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Anti-staphylococcal penicillins (ASPs) like nafcillin are considered first-line for MSSA bacteremia based on historical studies, but contemporary real-world data is limited. This dogma persists despite the frequent use of alternative beta-lactams (e.g., cefazolin, ceftriaxone) due to ASP intolerance, availability, or dosing convenience, without robust evidence of inferiority. A rigorous comparison is needed to inform evidence-based therapy, especially in diverse patient populations including those with oral step-down, as explored in Dr. Ortwine's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: For the treatment of MSSA bacteremia, alternative beta-lactam therapies (e.g., cefazolin) are non-inferior to anti-staphylococcal penicillins in terms of a composite clinical failure endpoint, after adjusting for key confounders including source of infection and illness severity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with MSSA bacteremia from 2018-2024 at three safety-net and tertiary care hospitals. The primary exposure is definitive antibiotic therapy categorized as ASP (nafcillin, oxacillin) or alternative beta-lactam (cefazolin, ceftriaxone). The primary outcome is a 30-day composite of all-cause mortality, bacteremia recurrence, or readmission. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders such as age, comorbidities, Pitt bacteremia score, infection source, and need for ICU care. A non-inferiority margin of 10% will be pre-specified.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, contemporary evidence to either reinforce or challenge the standard first-line role of ASPs for MSSA bacteremia. It will directly inform clinical guidelines and bedside decisions, particularly for patients where ASP use is problematic. The findings will also create a framework for evaluating oral step-down options, bridging Dr. Ortwine's previous research on oral therapy for streptococcal bacteremia.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared these agents, this work uniquely focuses on a heterogeneous, real-world cohort from safety-net and tertiary hospitals, capturing outcomes in patients often underrepresented in clinical trials, and employs rigorous modern statistical techniques to address confounding by indication.

proposals.title: Comparative Nephrotoxicity of Anti-staphylococcal Beta-lactams: A Retrospective Cohort Study of Nafcillin versus Cefazolin in Hospitalized Patients
proposals.motivation: Nafcillin and cefazolin are cornerstone therapies for methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) infections. Current treatment guidelines and clinical practice often consider them interchangeable, with selection influenced by dosing convenience and institutional preference, implicitly assuming comparable safety profiles, including nephrotoxicity risk. However, this assumption is not well-supported by robust comparative data, and clarifying any differential risk is crucial for optimizing patient safety, especially in vulnerable populations and amidst growing concerns about antibiotic-associated acute kidney injury (AKI).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the incidence of nephrotoxicity differs significantly between patients treated with nafcillin and those treated with cefazolin for serious MSSA infections, after adjusting for known confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity score-matched cohort study at a large safety-net hospital. Adult inpatients receiving ≥72 hours of intravenous nafcillin or cefazolin for MSSA bacteremia or deep-seated infection between 2018-2024 will be included. The primary outcome is nephrotoxicity, defined by the KDIGO serum creatinine criteria. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching (nearest-neighbor, caliper 0.2) to adjust for confounders including age, baseline renal function, concomitant nephrotoxins, sepsis severity, and vancomycin exposure. Secondary analyses will assess time-to-AKI and need for renal replacement therapy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, adjusted comparative safety analysis of these two key anti-staphylococcal agents, directly informing therapeutic decision-making. The results will guide clinicians in weighing nephrotoxicity risk when choosing between nafcillin and cefazolin, potentially influencing institutional protocols and stewardship guidelines to improve patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While the hepatotoxicity of nafcillin is documented, a focused, methodologically rigorous comparison of nephrotoxicity—a common reason for therapy alteration—between these first-line agents has not been performed, challenging a widely held clinical equivalence assumption.

proposals.title: Comparative Safety of Anti-MSSA Beta-Lactams: A Multi-Center Retrospective Cohort Study Assessing Class-Wide Adverse Event Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Beta-lactams are the cornerstone of therapy for methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) infections, but their safety profiles are often assumed to be uniformly favorable and similar across the class. This assumption drives empirical and definitive treatment choices, yet pre-2026 pharmacovigilance data and clinical experience suggest potential differences in adverse events like nephrotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, and hematologic effects. A rigorous, head-to-head safety comparison is needed to inform more precise, patient-centered antibiotic selection, especially in vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant heterogeneity exists in the safety profiles of commonly used anti-MSSA beta-lactams (e.g., cefazolin, oxacillin/nafcillin, flucloxacillin, and anti-staphylococcal penicillins combined with beta-lactamase inhibitors), and that cefazolin has a superior safety profile compared to anti-staphylococcal penicillins, particularly regarding nephrotoxicity and hepatotoxicity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of hospitalized adults treated for MSSA bloodstream infections or complicated bacteremia between 2018-2024. Using structured electronic health record data, we will define cohorts by definitive beta-lactam therapy. Primary outcomes will be composite safety events: acute kidney injury, significant liver enzyme elevation, and hematologic toxicity. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders like age, comorbidities, concomitant nephrotoxins, and illness severity. A secondary analysis will compare safety in high-risk subgroups, such as patients with COVID-19 or baseline renal dysfunction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, comparative safety analysis of anti-MSSA beta-lactams, moving beyond efficacy to directly address treatment tolerability. It will generate evidence to challenge or refine the class-wide safety assumption, potentially identifying a safer first-line agent. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship programs, aiding in the selection of therapies that minimize harm while maintaining efficacy against drug-resistant pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares efficacy (e.g., cefazolin vs. oxacillin), a systematic, multi-center evaluation focusing exclusively on heterogeneous safety outcomes within the beta-lactam class for MSSA is lacking. This proposal leverages real-world data from diverse, contemporary patient populations, including those impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, to test a foundational but unproven clinical axiom.

proposals.title: A Pharmacometric Model Linking Beta-Lactam Exposure to Clinical Outcomes in Methicillin-Susceptible Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia: A Translational Approach from Oncology
proposals.motivation: Methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) bacteremia remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality, with treatment failure rates of 20-30% despite appropriate antibiotic therapy. Current dosing strategies for beta-lactams are based on population averages and microbiological targets (e.g., time above MIC), but they inadequately account for interpatient variability in drug exposure and its link to hard clinical endpoints like mortality or relapse. This one-size-fits-all approach may contribute to suboptimal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pharmacometric model, using established exposure-response frameworks from oncology, can identify a patient-specific beta-lactam exposure threshold (e.g., a time above a multiple of the MIC) that is predictive of clinical cure and minimized toxicity in MSSA bacteremia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult patients with MSSA bacteremia treated with standard beta-lactams (e.g., cefazolin, oxacillin) at our institution. Using patient-level pharmacokinetic (PK) data (from therapeutic drug monitoring where available) or population PK models, we will estimate individual drug exposure metrics. We will then apply a time-to-event (survival) modeling framework, analogous to exposure-efficacy models in oncology, to link these exposure metrics to the time-to-clinical failure (a composite of mortality, persistent bacteremia, or relapse). A separate model will link exposure to nephrotoxicity. Model development will utilize non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) and parametric survival analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, patient-centric framework for optimizing beta-lactam therapy in MSSA bacteremia, moving beyond microbiological surrogates. It will generate specific, evidence-based exposure targets that can be prospectively validated and used to guide therapeutic drug monitoring and personalized dosing, potentially improving cure rates and reducing toxicity.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacometric modeling is standard in oncology, its application to define exposure-response relationships for common antibiotics like beta-lactams in serious infections is novel. This approach directly translates a sophisticated methodological paradigm from a different therapeutic area to address a persistent problem in infectious diseases.

proposals.title: Oral Stepdown Therapy for MSSA Bacteremia in High-Risk Subgroups: A Comparative Effectiveness Study in Immunocompromised and Elderly Patients
proposals.motivation: Oral antibiotic stepdown therapy for uncomplicated MSSA bacteremia is a strategy to reduce hospital length of stay, but its safety and efficacy in high-risk subgroups like the immunocompromised and elderly remain poorly defined. These patients have altered immune responses and higher risks of relapse and complications, making general trial results potentially inapplicable. Given the rising pressure on hospital capacity and the need for antimicrobial stewardship, evidence-based guidance for treating these vulnerable populations is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: In immunocompromised and elderly (≥75 years) patients with uncomplicated MSSA bacteremia, oral stepdown therapy (e.g., with a highly bioavailable agent like trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or a fluoroquinolone) is non-inferior to continued intravenous therapy for the composite endpoint of 90-day all-cause mortality or relapse, but is associated with a higher rate of drug-related adverse events.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, propensity score-matched cohort study at a large safety-net hospital system. Patients with MSSA bacteremia from 2018-2024 will be identified via electronic health record data extraction. High-risk subgroups will be defined as immunocompromised (solid organ transplant, active chemotherapy, high-dose steroids, advanced HIV) or age ≥75. Using logistic regression to generate propensity scores, patients receiving oral stepdown will be matched 1:1 to those receiving full-course IV therapy. The primary analysis will employ a Cox proportional hazards model to compare time-to-composite failure, with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 10%. Secondary analyses will compare adverse events, readmissions, and cost.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative effectiveness data on oral stepdown for MSSA bacteremia specifically tailored to high-risk populations, filling a critical evidence gap. The results will directly inform clinical guidelines and antimicrobial stewardship programs on the safe use of this treatment strategy in vulnerable patients, potentially reducing unnecessary hospitalization. It will also identify specific risk factors for failure within these subgroups to guide patient selection.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Ortwine's prior work studied oral stepdown in a general population, this proposal deliberately scales down to focus on the subgroups where clinicians are most hesitant to adopt this strategy, making the findings directly actionable for high-stakes decisions. The combined analysis of immunocompromised and elderly patients in a single, risk-stratified framework is novel and addresses a common clinical dilemma.

proposals.title: Predictors of Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Treatment Failure: A Machine Learning Analysis of Host and Pathogen Factors in Hospital-Acquired Infections
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a pre-2026 global health crisis, often framed as a problem of antibiotic choice. However, clinical failures occur even with appropriate, in vitro active agents, suggesting underlying host or infection factors are critical. Identifying predictors of failure independent of specific antibiotic selection could enable earlier intervention and more personalized risk stratification, moving beyond the current paradigm of escalating antibiotic regimens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite of host immune status, infection source, and specific pathogen virulence factors, rather than antibiotic susceptibility alone, will be the primary predictors of treatment failure in serious bacterial infections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients (2018-2024) with culture-positive hospital-acquired pneumonia or bloodstream infections treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., carbapenems, anti-MRSA agents). The primary outcome is composite treatment failure (persistent symptoms, microbiologic failure, escalation of therapy, or 30-day mortality). We will train and validate a gradient boosting machine (XGBoost) model using features including host factors (e.g., SOFA score, immunocompromise), infection characteristics (source, bacteremia), and pathogen data (species, intrinsic resistance profile, but not drug-specific MICs). Model interpretation will use SHAP values to identify key predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable risk score for early identification of patients at high risk of failing standard antibiotic therapy. It will shift the focus from pathogen-centric to patient-centric predictors of poor outcome, informing trial design for adjunctive therapies and stewardship programs aimed at high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: Unlike most pre-2026 models that predict resistance to a specific drug, this study explicitly excludes antibiotic choice as a primary variable to uncover failure predictors common across regimens, addressing a fundamental inverse problem in infectious diseases.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Dalbavancin vs. Outpatient Parenteral Antibiotic Therapy (OPAT) for High-Risk Patients with Gram-Positive Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Dalbavancin, a long-acting lipoglycopeptide, is approved for acute bacterial skin infections but is increasingly used off-label for serious Gram-positive infections, primarily in patients deemed ineligible for OPAT due to psychosocial barriers. This practice reinforces a paradigm where dalbavancin is a 'consolation' therapy. Given the high clinical failure rates and logistical burdens of OPAT, especially for high-risk patients with comorbidities or substance use, a direct comparison is needed to determine if dalbavancin's simplified, single-dose regimen offers superior outcomes for all high-risk patients, not just the OPAT-ineligible.
proposals.hypothesis: For high-risk patients with Gram-positive bloodstream infections (e.g., due to S. aureus, including MRSA), treatment with dalbavancin will result in higher rates of clinical cure at 30 days and lower rates of hospital readmission compared to standard OPAT regimens.
proposals.method: We will conduct an open-label, randomized controlled trial at a safety-net hospital. High-risk adults with uncomplicated Gram-positive bacteremia will be randomized 1:1 to receive either a two-dose dalbavancin regimen (1500mg on day 1, 1500mg on day 8) or a standard OPAT regimen (e.g., vancomycin, daptomycin). The primary outcome is clinical cure at 30-day follow-up. Secondary outcomes include 90-day readmission, adverse events, and patient satisfaction. Statistical analysis will use intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses, with logistic regression to adjust for confounders like pathogen and comorbidity burden.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence comparing dalbavancin to OPAT in a high-risk population, directly informing treatment guidelines for serious Gram-positive infections. It will quantify the potential benefits of long-acting lipoglycopeptides in improving outcomes and healthcare utilization beyond their current niche role, potentially reshaping the treatment pathway for vulnerable patient groups.
proposals.novelty: Existing studies position dalbavancin as an alternative for OPAT-ineligible patients; this trial directly challenges that framework by testing its superiority as a first-line strategy for all high-risk patients, a concept not evaluated in prior randomized trials.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Single-Dose vs. Multi-Dose Oral Antibiotic Regimens for High-Risk, Uncomplicated Gram-Negative Bacteriuria in Hospitalized Patients: A Pragmatic, Non-Inferiority Trial
proposals.motivation: The drive for antimicrobial stewardship and patient convenience has increased the use of single-dose antibiotic therapies, particularly for uncomplicated infections. However, the efficacy of this approach in hospitalized patients with complex comorbidities or infections caused by pathogens with higher minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) is uncertain, potentially leading to treatment failure and relapse. This study addresses a critical gap in applying simplified regimens to a vulnerable inpatient population where pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic challenges are more pronounced.
proposals.hypothesis: For hospitalized patients with uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteriuria and at least one high-risk feature (e.g., diabetes, mild renal impairment, or infection with a non-E. coli species), a multi-dose oral antibiotic regimen (e.g., 3-5 days) will be superior to a single-dose regimen in achieving clinical and microbiological cure by test-of-cure visit, and the convenience benefit of single-dose therapy will not outweigh the risk of inferior outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial at a safety-net hospital. Hospitalized adults with uncomplicated Gram-negative bacteriuria (≥10^5 CFU/mL) and a high-risk feature will be randomized 1:1 to receive either a single dose of oral fosfomycin trometamol (3g) or a multi-dose regimen (e.g., oral nitrofurantoin 100mg BID for 5 days). The primary outcome is composite clinical and microbiological cure at day 14. Secondary outcomes include 30-day recurrence, adverse events, and patient-reported convenience scores. Statistical analysis will employ a modified intention-to-treat approach, with a pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 10%.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-quality, patient-centered evidence to guide antibiotic duration decisions for a common infection in a complex inpatient setting, directly informing stewardship protocols. It will quantify the trade-off between regimen convenience and clinical effectiveness, helping to define the boundaries where single-dose therapy remains appropriate versus where extended therapy is warranted.
proposals.novelty: While single-dose therapy is established for young, healthy outpatients, this study rigorously tests its limits in a hospitalized, comorbid population—a group often excluded from prior pivotal trials. It moves beyond the dogma of 'single-dose is always preferable' by prospectively evaluating a nuanced, risk-stratified approach to treatment duration.

ideas.title: Applying Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) Indices from Antibiotic Development to Guide Pathogen-Specific Treatment Duration
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from clinical pharmacology, where antibiotic dosing is optimized using pathogen-specific PK/PD indices. The analogous problem—determining the right drug exposure for different bugs—was solved by establishing target values for indices like %T>MIC or AUC/MIC. We adapt this by investigating whether the recommended ~7-day treatment duration is optimal for all Gram-negative organisms. The adapted idea is to move beyond a one-size-fits-all duration by defining pathogen-specific PK/PD targets for treatment cessation. For key pathogens like *E. coli*, *K. pneumoniae*, and *P. aeruginosa*, we would use in vitro and in vivo infection models to identify the precise drug exposure profile required to ensure eradication and prevent resistance, thereby creating a framework for duration guidance based on achieving a measurable, pathogen-specific pharmacological endpoint rather than a fixed time period.

ideas.title: An Adaptive Platform Trial to Simultaneously Optimize Antibiotic Strategy and Source Investigation Protocol for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
ideas.description: This study employs an adaptive platform trial design to concurrently evaluate multiple antibiotic durations/agents and two competing diagnostic strategies (mandatory standardized imaging vs. selective imaging based on clinical criteria) for Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infection (GNBSI). The adaptive design allows for dropping ineffective antibiotic arms and, crucially, for modifying the imaging allocation ratio based on interim analyses of which patient subgroups (e.g., defined by pathogen, presentation, or early response) derive the greatest benefit from mandatory source investigation. This integrated approach efficiently identifies the optimal combination of diagnostic workup and antimicrobial therapy, tailoring both to patient characteristics to improve outcomes and resource use.

ideas.title: Pediatric physiologically-based PK/PD modeling to define and optimize oral step-down therapy for uncomplicated GNBSI
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the precision of pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling with the specific clinical need for pediatric adaptation. It aims to develop pediatric physiologically-based PK/PD models for key Gram-negative pathogens to simulate drug exposure across age groups. These models will be used to identify PK/PD targets predictive of cure for oral antibiotics, thereby generating the pharmacological evidence needed to define pediatric-specific criteria for 'uncomplicated' GNBSI eligible for oral step-down. The ultimate goal is to create optimized, age-stratified oral dosing regimens that maximize efficacy and safety while supporting antimicrobial stewardship in children.

ideas.title: An adaptive platform trial for oral step-down therapy in uncomplicated GNBSI, dynamically updating regimens based on resistance and efficacy data
ideas.description: This research establishes a perpetual, multi-center adaptive platform trial to generate contemporary evidence for oral step-down in Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). The trial uses a master protocol with a shared control group and employs response-adaptive randomization to favor more effective regimens, while underperforming or obsolete regimens are dropped based on interim analyses. New oral antibiotic regimens can be incorporated into the platform as they become clinically available, ensuring the evidence base remains aligned with current antimicrobial resistance landscapes and therapeutic options.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control for Antibiotic De-escalation: A Model-Predictive Approach from Aerospace Engineering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the model-predictive control (MPC) solution from aerospace engineering to solve the clinical dual problem of optimizing de-escalation timing in Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). In aerospace, MPC uses a dynamic model and cost function to determine the optimal point to switch between control modes (e.g., ascent to cruise) for efficient, stable system performance. The adapted research would develop a patient-specific predictive model using serial clinical and biomarker data. By defining a cost function that quantifies the trade-offs between treatment failure and excessive broad-spectrum use, the study would identify not a static threshold, but an optimal *predictive switching rule*. This rule would determine when to initiate the switch from broad to narrow-spectrum therapy *in anticipation* of reaching a clinical endpoint, thereby optimizing the trajectory of care for both individual outcomes and population-level antibiotic stewardship.

ideas.title: Precision Duration Signatures: A Multi-Domain Model Integrating Pathogen, Host, and Drug Dynamics
ideas.description: This research moves beyond pathogen-specific rules to develop precision duration guidance based on integrated patient-pathogen-treatment signatures. It builds a predictive model using high-resolution data from clinical cohorts, including bacterial genomic and transcriptomic features, host immune and inflammatory markers, site-of-infection drug concentrations, and resistance phenotypes. The model's output is a validated, dynamic algorithm that recommends treatment duration based on the composite profile's predicted time to sustained microbiological and clinical resolution, aiming to minimize both relapse and unnecessary antibiotic exposure.

ideas.title: Physiology-Informed, Dynamic PK/PD Modeling for Precision Oral Step-Down Therapy in GNBSI
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a dynamic, physiology-informed pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling platform to optimize oral step-down therapy for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). The platform integrates population PK data with mechanistic models of key recovery-phase variables—such as resolving systemic inflammation, improving renal/hepatic function, and normalizing gastrointestinal motility—that are absent from standard drug development models. By using longitudinal clinical and biomarker data from individual patients, the system generates personalized, time-dependent exposure predictions to guide both the timing and dosing of oral antibiotics. This approach directly addresses the pathophysiological mismatch of static models, aiming to maximize efficacy and minimize toxicity and treatment failure during the critical transition from intravenous to oral therapy.

ideas.title: Prospective Derivation and Validation of a Clinical-Microbiological Risk Score for Metastatic Complications in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infection
ideas.description: This study aims to develop and validate a predictive tool for identifying patients with GNBSI at highest risk for complicated courses. It prospectively collects standardized clinical (e.g., quick SOFA, source control status, comorbidities), microbiological (e.g., species, genotypic resistance markers), and biomarker (e.g., procalcitonin) data at diagnosis within a multi-center cohort. The primary outcome is a composite of metastatic complications, relapse, or infection-related mortality. Using this data, a multivariate model will generate a risk score to objectively stratify patients at presentation, guiding early decisions on therapy duration and monitoring intensity.

ideas.title: Adapting Radiation Fractionation Principles to Antibiotic Duration
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a universal 7-day antibiotic duration for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) by drawing an analogy from radiation oncology's successful challenge of conventional fractionation. In that field, the solution involved using biological dose modeling and strict patient selection to prove non-inferiority of shorter, higher-dose-per-session (hypofractionated) regimens. Adapted here, the research would first use PK/PD principles to model a 5-day antibiotic course predicted to deliver a 'biologically effective dose' equivalent to 7 days for a precisely defined uncomplicated GNBSI population. A prospective, randomized controlled non-inferiority trial would then test this optimized shorter course, with the primary endpoint being clinical cure at a defined test-of-cure visit, mirroring the paradigm-shifting methodology of radiation oncology fractionation trials.

ideas.title: Applying Pest Management Economic Injury Level Models to Optimize Oral Step-Down Therapy for GNBSI
ideas.description: This idea applies the core principle of Economic Injury Level (EIL) modeling from agricultural pest management to the optimization of oral step-down dosing for Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). In the analogous field, EIL models mathematically define the pest population threshold at which the cost of damage equals the cost of control, enabling precise, minimal intervention. The adapted solution translates this into a 'Clinical Injury Level' framework for pathogens. By integrating pathogen PK/PD (growth rates, kill curves) with clinical outcome data (risk of relapse based on residual burden), the model would identify the optimal oral regimen—dose and duration—that maintains pathogen suppression below the injury threshold while minimizing drug exposure, analogous to using the minimal effective pesticide application to protect economic yield.

ideas.title: Pediatric GNBSI Host-Response Phenotyping for Dynamic Management
ideas.description: This research fuses pediatric adaptation of GNBSI guidelines with a biomarker-based definition of complexity. It proposes to prospectively validate a panel of dynamic host-response biomarkers (e.g., PCT, CRP trajectories, specific cytokines, cell-surface markers) in children with GNBSI to define 'uncomplicated' infection not by checklist, but by a rapid return to immune homeostasis. The core innovation is developing and validating a pediatric-specific, biomarker-driven algorithm that dynamically risk-stratifies patients and personalizes decisions on antibiotic duration and de-escalation, potentially shortening therapy for 'rapid resolvers' and intensifying management for those with a dysregulated host response despite benign clinical appearance.

ideas.title: Co-design and validate context-adapted management algorithms for resource-limited settings
ideas.description: This research begins with an intensive contextual analysis phase, using mixed methods including focus groups with frontline health workers, supply chain audits, and analysis of local surveillance data to understand the true diagnostic and therapeutic landscape. Researchers then collaborate with local clinicians and health officials to iteratively design a syndrome-based management algorithm that is specifically adapted to local resource constraints, antibiotic availability, and prevalent pathogens. The feasibility and effectiveness of this co-designed algorithm are then rigorously tested in a stepped-wedge or cluster-randomized trial, with primary outcomes focusing on patient-centered clinical resolution and secondary outcomes measuring algorithm adherence and system-level barriers.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Triage Protocols for Clinical Decision Support in Resource-Limited Settings
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from conservation biology, specifically ecological triage protocols used in resource-limited wildlife management, to the challenge of scaling a clinical consensus approach in settings with limited diagnostics and antibiotics. The analogous solution uses simple, field-applicable decision matrices based on key observable criteria to guide action systematically without complex tools. Adapted to the medical context, the research would involve developing and testing a simplified clinical decision support tool—a 'treatment triage matrix'—that operationalizes the consensus approach. This tool would use a structured algorithm based on syndromic presentation and local drug availability to guide empiric treatment decisions, enabling the deployment of the consensus strategy in global resource-limited settings to improve antibiotic stewardship and patient outcomes where laboratory support is scarce.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Patient-Specific De-escalation Policies for GNBSI via Stochastic Decision Modeling
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the search for an optimal de-escalation threshold with formal decision analysis by constructing a stochastic, patient-level decision model. The model will incorporate dynamic biomarkers (e.g., PCT, clinical scores) as time-varying state variables within a Markov decision process or discrete-event simulation framework. This allows for the evaluation and identification of optimal, adaptive de-escalation policies that specify not just a single threshold, but a rule set dictating when to switch therapy based on a patient's evolving risk profile. The outcome is a validated, model-derived clinical decision support tool that maximizes cost-utility by balancing the risks of prolonged broad-spectrum use against the risks of premature de-escalation for individual patients with Gram-negative bloodstream infections.

ideas.title: A dynamic, closed-loop risk assessment and monitoring system for short-course GNBSI therapy
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive clinical decision support system that begins with a baseline prediction score for short-course therapy failure risk. The system's core innovation is a dynamic monitoring protocol that integrates real-time patient data—including early clinical response, trends in inflammatory biomarkers, and pathogen-specific factors—to continuously reassess risk throughout the treatment period. This closed-loop design allows for pre-emptive identification of patients who are deviating from the expected response pathway, enabling timely intervention. The final output is a pragmatic, adaptive tool that moves beyond one-time stratification to active, personalized management of therapy duration.

ideas.title: Risk-Adapted vs. Universal Imaging for Occult Source Identification in GNBSI
ideas.description: This study develops and validates a clinical prediction rule to stratify patients with GNBSI by their risk of having an occult source requiring intervention. It then conducts a multi-center, pragmatic trial comparing three strategies: 1) a mandatory universal imaging protocol, 2) a purely selective approach based on clinician judgment, and 3) a novel risk-adapted protocol where imaging intensity is guided by the patient's risk stratum. The primary outcome is a composite measure of net clinical benefit, incorporating timely source identification and intervention, balanced against harms from unnecessary imaging and procedures. Secondary outcomes include cost-effectiveness, mortality, and recurrence rates, aiming to define an evidence-based, efficient standard of care.

ideas.title: A Tiered Host-Response Framework for Actionable GNBSI Complexity Classification
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a clinical decision system that translates dynamic host-response biomarkers into a standardized, actionable classification for Gram-negative bloodstream infection (GNBSI) complexity. It focuses on a minimal panel of rapid, reproducible biomarkers whose kinetic profiles feed into a validated algorithm, producing a tiered clinical output (e.g., uncomplicated, evolving, complicated). This approach directly addresses the need for biologically informed precision while maintaining the practical utility required for bedside decision-making regarding treatment duration and source control. The final output is a pragmatic tool that inverts the traditional definition by making the dynamic host response the primary, but interpretable, driver of complexity assessment.

ideas.title: Apply Portfolio Optimization from Financial Engineering to GNBSI Management Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts the portfolio optimization solution from financial engineering to the problem of managing Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). In finance, the Markowitz mean-variance model solves the problem of selecting an optimal mix of assets by formally balancing expected return against risk. Analogously, in GNBSI management, various intervention pathways (e.g., different empiric antibiotics, rapid diagnostics, stewardship rules) can be modeled as assets, with their expected health utility (QALYs) as return and outcome variance (from clinical uncertainty) as risk. The adapted research idea formalizes GNBSI management into a portfolio optimization model to identify the optimal cost-utility strategy mix that maximizes expected clinical benefit for a given acceptable level of outcome risk or resource investment, providing a more robust framework for decision-making under uncertainty than a standard decision tree alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Structural Health Monitoring for Clinical Risk Stratification in GNBSI
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Structural Health Monitoring in civil engineering to the clinical problem of risk-stratifying patients with Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI) for short-course therapy failure. The analogous field uses integrated sensor data and digital twin models to predict structural failure by identifying critical stress patterns. The adapted idea is to develop a dynamic, multi-parameter clinical prediction tool that functions as a 'clinical digital twin.' This tool would integrate serial host-response (e.g., biomarkers, transcriptomics), pathogen (e.g., genomic virulence factors, MICs), and clinical data to continuously compute a risk score. The model would identify the specific, evolving combination of factors that signals impending treatment failure, moving beyond static admission scores to enable real-time, personalized therapy escalation or de-escalation.

ideas.title: Causal Machine Learning for Dynamic, Generalizable Risk Prediction in Gram-Negative Bloodstream Infections
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic risk prediction system for GNBSI complications by integrating causal inference methodologies with machine learning on longitudinal EHR data. The approach uses structural causal models and targeted maximum likelihood estimation to adjust for time-dependent confounding and estimate the causal effect of patient trajectories on complication risk. This ensures the model learns stable, mechanistic relationships rather than non-causal associations, significantly improving its reliability and generalizability across clinical settings. The final system provides real-time, interpretable risk scores that are robust to shifts in data distribution and clinical practice.

ideas.title: Adaptive Platform Trial for GNBSI using Multi-Armed Bandit Algorithms
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from machine learning—specifically, reinforcement learning's multi-armed bandit problem—to the clinical trial design challenge. In the analogous field, algorithms like Thompson sampling dynamically balance exploration and exploitation to efficiently learn the best option among many. Adapted to the research context of GNBSI, the platform trial would use such an algorithm as its core adaptive engine. It would continuously analyze interim outcome data (e.g., clinical cure, mortality, resistance emergence) to probabilistically allocate incoming patients to the various antibiotic durations and agents being tested. This creates a self-optimizing trial that maximizes patient benefit during the study, accelerates the identification of optimal regimens, and uses resources more efficiently than traditional or simpler adaptive designs.

ideas.title: Adaptive ML for GNBSI Risk using Dynamic Portfolio Risk Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational finance, where ML models perform real-time, dynamic risk prediction for financial portfolios using continuously updating data streams. The analogous solution uses temporal models like RNNs to ingest high-frequency market data and predict metrics like Value-at-Risk. Adapted to the clinical context, we propose using similar temporal ML architectures to ingest longitudinal EHR data streams (vitals, labs, medications) for patients with Gram-negative bloodstream infections (GNBSI). The model would dynamically predict a continuously updated risk score for complications (e.g., septic shock, organ failure), functioning as a real-time clinical risk dashboard that alerts to sudden deteriorations, much like a trading system flags emerging portfolio risks.

ideas.title: Adapting a Consensus-Building Delphi Method from Software Engineering for Patient-Centered Outcome Definition
ideas.description: This idea adapts a consensus-building method from software engineering's requirements gathering phase to the clinical research context. In software engineering, a modified Delphi process is used to converge diverse expert stakeholders (developers, users, managers) on critical, often qualitative, system requirements like usability and performance. The analogous solution is adapted by treating patients as the primary 'expert stakeholders' in defining what constitutes a meaningful treatment outcome. The adapted research idea employs a structured, multi-round Delphi process where a patient panel anonymously rates and refines potential outcome measures. Through iterative rounds with controlled feedback summarizing the group's views, the process aims to achieve consensus on a patient-centered core outcome set for GNBSI treatment trials, ensuring the trials measure what matters most to those living with the condition.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Triage for GNBSI: Adapting Structural Engineering's Load-Bearing Software for Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from structural engineering, where algorithmic software has relaxed the need for specialist consensus for routine calculations. The analogous solution involves certified software that embeds expert rules and safety codes, enabling non-specialists to make sound technical decisions. Adapted to the GNBSI context, the research would involve developing a guideline-based clinical decision support algorithm. A prospective, randomized controlled trial would then test if algorithm-driven management by non-specialist clinicians yields equivalent patient outcomes to traditional expert-guided management, challenging the assumption that specialist consensus is always necessary for standard cases.

ideas.title: Dynamic Host-Response Phenotyping for GNBSI Using Ecological Resilience Metrics
ideas.description: This idea inverts the definition of 'uncomplicated' GNBSI by challenging the assumption that static clinical criteria are sufficient. Drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology, where system health is assessed via dynamic resilience metrics rather than static species lists, the solution is adapted. We propose to define GNBSI complexity based on dynamic biomarkers of host response, creating a 'host-response resilience index' from serial measurements. This index, quantifying the rate and stability of the return to physiological homeostasis, would replace binary clinical checklists, offering a continuous, biologically grounded measure of infection complexity and patient vulnerability.

The system is designed to iteratively update its parameters by assimilating real-time local data on resistance, resource availability, and patient flow. This creates a robust, adaptive tool that identifies optimal management strategies tailored to a hospital's evolving epidemiological and operational state.
